meta profile sovereign system, that is fully local, and respects users privacy and helps to actually own and control his data about his network of contacts, connections and partners.\n\nFeatures:\n- Import/export with unified database and external services (via API and bulk export archive files)\n- Import/export with unified database and .lino files\n- Unified database is stored as both in binary form and text form, for reliability and express export\n- Regular backups of unified database in archive target folder\n- Unified chat UI to support chats in all external services\n- Auto-completion in chat UI based on previous messages from yourself in history\n- Operator UI, that automatically switches between chats/contexts, allowing to focus on efficient manual processing of each unread message based on ideas from https://github.com/link-assistant/operator\n- Patterns editor - allow to automatically infer/constract/simplify/generalize regular expressions and PEGs to match multiple messages. User can use one or multiple messages, and fuzzy search to find candidates from the history, once found user can select to add them pattern to match wider range of messages.\n- Reply message variation group editor, we should use fuzzy search to extract typical replies to the same question or message from the history of own messages, user can add manually as many variations as he wants.\n- Automated scripts, that handle typical messages like hi/hello, thank you, and so on. UI should be similar to n8n, with dependencies between patterns and reply variations. That should visualize typical dialogs tree from history, so that user can configure how to automatically control multi stage conversations, when multiple questions need to be asked once previous answers are gotten. So routing talking, first contact, knowing each other can be automated. It should be run in fully automated more or semi automated mode. In semi automated mode user can see that system detected some condition it can react to, and either manually select reply variation or confirm random selections as it would in fully automated mode.\n- We should be able to use pattern matching on multiple messages, to scan conversations for personal private facts we got to know about each user, to help the user remember all facts where discussed in the each conversation without looking too much at it, saving time. All personal data captured from conversation automatically should be shown in form `question - answer`, for both sides you and your chat partner. For chats with multiple users we should be able to see what questions was answered by each chat participant.\n- CRM features like ability to see in one place what we know about our contacts/friends in one place, ability cross reference, intersect sets of chats, groups, communites, and facts to select target audience and send them all hi/hello or stickers, to start massive manual conversations with only relevant and target audience\n- Ability to search locally for people, communities, companies, messages, chats, with configurable parameters\n- Unified broadcasing UI to support public posting on walls/public posts in all services\n- Profile sync in all services\n- Resumes sync in all services\n- External services support: VK, Telegram, X, WhatsApp, Facebook, Linked-In, career.habr.com, hh.ru, superjob.ru\n- Fully open-source and public domain requiring no trust\n- Every function and feature should be importable as npm library\n- NPM library should contain CLI interface, and electron desktop interface, ability to start web server to connect to local storage\n- All clients should allow sync of the data between them using WebRTC mapped to localstorage or any configurable endpoint, if user wants to host it in the internet to simplify the access from small devices like mobile phones, with ability to work with full local database in personal cloud.\n- Docker microservice for WebRTC server and for web server.\n- We should be able to do universal Links access interface in both server and client. On client like web it should combine access to local cache of data, and direct server access. On server it should combine access to both read and write at the same time to binary storage (link-cli/doublets-rs) and text storage (links notation).\n- We should use JavaScript on server and client and all UI apps with React.js, with Rust + WebAssembly for heavy workloads.\n- We should also have a version of server and microservice fully written in only Rust, but as well in JavaScript.\n- Application should use all the best practices from https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines, https://design.google, and Microsoft design guildelines.\n- The code should be written in simple way with no premature optimizations \n- All code should be documented with automated API documentation generation\n- We should cover all tests fully with unit, integration, and e2e tests using http://github.com/link-foundation/browser-commander\n\nFor all data exchanges and local text data-source (we should prefer indented format to minimize brackets/parentheses):\n- https://github.com/link-foundation/links-notation\n- https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-objects-codec\n\nFor all binary data in mobile, web, desktop, and in CLI/service (if any packages are not ready yet, we can use dependencies on source code directly):\n- http://github.com/link-foundation/link-cli\n- http://github.com/linksplatform/doublets-rs\n- http://github.com/linksplatform/doublets-web\n\nFor CLI arguments and configuration:\n- https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-arguments\n\nFor cross-platform apps (iOS, Android, Electron):\n- http://github.com/deep-foundation/sdk\n\nWe should reuse all features from:\n- https://github.com/konard/broadcast\n- https://github.com/konard/vk-bot\n- https://github.com/konard/vk\n- https://github.com/konard/vk-export\n- https://github.com/konard/vk-browser\n- https://github.com/konard/telegram-bot\n- https://github.com/konard/follow\n- https://github.com/konard/github-pages-telegram-mini-app\n- https://github.com/konard/telegramify-markdown\n\nChat UI quality as in:\n- https://github.com/drklo/telegram\n- https://github.com/telegramdesktop/tdesktop\n\nAutomated messaging scripts network UI quality as in:\n- https://n8n.io\n\nUse all the best practices from CI/CD templates (check full file tree to compare for all GitHub workflow and CI/CD scripts file), if the same issue is found in template report issue also in templates:\n- https://github.com/link-foundation/js-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template\n- https://github.com/link-foundation/rust-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template\n\nWe should compare all files, so we don't have more CI/CD errors in the future and reuse all the best practices from these templates.\n\nWe need to collect data related about the issue to this repository, make sure we compile that data [2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you see screenshots or images in issue descriptions, pull request descriptions, comments, or discussions, download the image to a local file first, then use the Read tool to view and analyze it. Before reading downloaded images with the Read tool, verify that the file is a valid image rather than HTML by using a CLI tool such as the 'file' command. When corrupted or non-image files, such as GitHub \"Not Found\" pages saved as `.png`, are read, they can cause \"Could not process image\" errors and crash the AI solver process. When the file command shows \"HTML\", \"text\", or \"ASCII text\", the download failed, so do not call Read on that file. Instead: (1) when images are from GitHub issues or PRs, such as URLs containing \"github.com/user-attachments\", retry with: curl -L -H \"Authorization: token \$(gh auth token)\" -o <filename> \"<url>\" (2) when the retry still fails, skip the image and note that it was unavailable.

[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you are fixing a bug, find the actual root cause first and run as many experiments as needed.

[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you need comments on a pull request, note that GitHub has three different comment types with different API endpoints:
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]       1. PR review comments (inline code comments): gh api repos/link-foundation/meta-sovereign/pulls/2/comments --paginate
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]       2. PR conversation comments (general discussion): gh api repos/link-foundation/meta-sovereign/issues/2/comments --paginate
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]       3. PR reviews (approve/request changes): gh api repos/link-foundation/meta-sovereign/pulls/2/reviews --paginate
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]       Note: The command \"gh pr view --json comments\" only returns conversation comments and misses review comments.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you need the latest comments on the issue, use gh api repos/link-foundation/meta-sovereign/issues/1/comments --paginate.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO] Solution development and testing.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When issue is solvable, first create a test that reproduces the problem, then implement the fix.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When implementing features, search for similar existing implementations in the codebase and use them as examples instead of implementing everything from scratch.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When coding, commit each atomic step that is useful on its own to the pull request branch so interrupted work remains preserved in the pull request.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you test:
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]       start from testing of small functions using separate scripts;
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]       write unit tests with mocks for easy and quick start.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you test integrations, use existing framework.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you test solution draft, include automated checks in pr.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you write or modify tests, consider setting reasonable timeouts at test, suite, and CI job levels so failures surface quickly instead of hanging.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you see repeated test timeout patterns in CI, investigate the root cause rather than increasing timeouts.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When the issue is unclear, write a comment on the issue with questions.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you encounter problems that you cannot solve yourself and need human help, write a comment on the pull request asking for help.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you need human help, use gh pr comment 2 --body \"your message\" to comment on existing PR.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO] Reproducible testing.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When fixing a bug, create a test that reproduces the problem before implementing the fix. When you cannot reproduce the problem, you cannot verify the fix.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When encountering logic bugs, write an automated test that fails due to the bug, then implement the fix to make it pass.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When encountering UI bugs, capture a screenshot showing the problem state, then create a visual regression test or manual verification screenshot after the fix.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When creating tests, prefer minimum reproducible examples, meaning the simplest test case that demonstrates the issue.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When submitting a fix, include in the PR description: (1) how to reproduce the issue, (2) the automated test that verifies the fix, (3) before/after screenshots for UI issues.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When a bug fix does not have a reproducing test, treat the fix as incomplete because regressions can occur later without notice.

[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]       check that no previously existing features were removed without an explicit request in the issue description, issue comments, or pull request comments.

[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO] GitHub CLI command patterns.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When fetching lists from GitHub API, use the --paginate flag to ensure all results are returned (GitHub returns max 30 per page by default).
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When listing PR review comments (inline code comments), use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/pulls/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When listing PR conversation comments, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When listing PR reviews, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/pulls/NUMBER/reviews --paginate.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When listing issue comments, use gh api repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/NUMBER/comments --paginate.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When adding PR comment, use gh pr comment NUMBER --body \"text\" --repo OWNER/REPO.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When adding issue comment, use gh issue comment NUMBER --body \"text\" --repo OWNER/REPO.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When viewing PR details, use gh pr view NUMBER --repo OWNER/REPO.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When filtering with jq, use gh api repos/\${owner}/\${repo}/pulls/\${prNumber}/comments --paginate --jq 'reverse | .[0:5]'.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO] Playwright MCP usage (browser automation via mcp__playwright__* tools).
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you develop frontend web applications (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, React, Vue, Angular, etc.), use Playwright MCP tools to test the UI in a real browser.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When WebFetch tool fails to retrieve expected content (e.g., returns empty content, JavaScript-rendered pages, or login-protected pages), use Playwright MCP tools (browser_navigate, browser_snapshot) as a fallback for web browsing.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When WebSearch tool fails or returns insufficient results, use Playwright MCP tools (browser_navigate, browser_snapshot) as a fallback for internet search.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you need to interact with dynamic web pages that require JavaScript execution, use Playwright MCP tools.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you need to visually verify how a web page looks or take screenshots, use browser_take_screenshot from Playwright MCP.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you need to fill forms, click buttons, or perform user interactions on web pages, use Playwright MCP tools (browser_click, browser_type, browser_fill_form).
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you need to test responsive design or different viewport sizes, use browser_resize from Playwright MCP.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you finish using the browser, close it with browser_close to free resources.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When reproducing UI bugs, use browser_take_screenshot to capture the problem state before implementing any fix.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When fixing UI bugs, take before/after screenshots to provide visual evidence of the fix for human verification.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When creating UI tests, save baseline screenshots to the repository for visual regression testing.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When verifying UI fixes, compare screenshots to ensure the fix does not introduce unintended visual changes.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO] Visual UI work and screenshots.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you work on visual UI changes (frontend, CSS, HTML, design), include a render or screenshot of the final result in the pull request description.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you need to show visual results, take a screenshot and save it to the repository (e.g., in a docs/screenshots/ or assets/ folder).
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When you save screenshots to the repository, use permanent links in the pull request description markdown (e.g., https://github.com/link-foundation/meta-sovereign/blob/issue-1-fc41adad29ce/docs/screenshots/result.png?raw=true).
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When uploading images, commit them to the branch first, then reference them using the GitHub blob URL format with ?raw=true suffix (works for both public and private repositories).
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When the visual result is important for review, mention it explicitly in the pull request description with the embedded image.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When fixing UI bugs, capture both the \"before\" (problem) and \"after\" (fixed) screenshots as evidence for human verification.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When reporting UI bugs, include a screenshot of the problem state to enable visual verification of the fix.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When the fix is visual, include side-by-side or sequential comparison of before/after states in the PR description.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.977Z] [INFO]    - When possible, create automated visual regression tests to prevent the UI bug from recurring." | jq -c .)

[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO] General guidelines.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]    - When you execute commands and the output becomes large, save the logs to files for easier review.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]    - When running commands, avoid setting a timeout yourself. Let them run as long as needed. The default timeout of 2 minutes is usually enough, and once commands finish, review the logs in the file.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]    - When running sudo commands, especially package installations like apt-get, yum, or npm install, run them in the background to avoid timeout issues and permission errors when the process needs to be killed. Use the run_in_background parameter or append & to the command.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]    - When CI is failing or user reports failures, consider adding a detailed investigation protocol to your todo list with these steps:
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]       Step 1: List recent runs with timestamps using: gh run list --repo link-foundation/meta-sovereign --branch issue-1-fc41adad29ce --limit 5 --json databaseId,conclusion,createdAt,headSha
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]       Step 2: Verify runs are after the latest commit by checking timestamps and SHA
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]       Step 3: For each non-passing run, download logs to preserve them: gh run view {run-id} --repo link-foundation/meta-sovereign --log > ci-logs/{workflow}-{run-id}.log
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]       Step 4: Read each downloaded log file with the Read tool to understand the actual failures
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]       Step 5: Report findings with specific errors and line numbers from logs
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]       This detailed investigation is especially helpful when user mentions CI failures, asks to investigate logs, you see non-passing status, or when finalizing a PR.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]       Note: If user says "failing" but tools show "passing", this might indicate stale data - consider downloading fresh logs and checking timestamps to resolve the discrepancy.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]    - When a code or log file has more than 1500 lines, read it in chunks of 1500 lines.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]    - When facing a complex problem, do as much tracing as possible and turn on all verbose modes.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]    - When you create debug, test, or example scripts while fixing an issue, keep them in ./examples and/or ./experiments so you can reuse them later.

[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]    - When you read the issue, read all details and comments thoroughly.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]    - When you see screenshots or images in issue descriptions, pull request descriptions, comments, or discussions, download the image to a local file first, then use the Read tool to view and analyze it. Before reading downloaded images with the Read tool, verify that the file is a valid image rather than HTML by using a CLI tool such as the 'file' command. When corrupted or non-image files, such as GitHub "Not Found" pages saved as `.png`, are read, they can cause "Could not process image" errors and crash the AI solver process. When the file command shows "HTML", "text", or "ASCII text", the download failed, so do not call Read on that file. Instead: (1) when images are from GitHub issues or PRs, such as URLs containing "github.com/user-attachments", retry with: curl -L -H "Authorization: token $(gh auth token)" -o <filename> "<url>" (2) when the retry still fails, skip the image and note that it was unavailable.

[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]    - When adding PR comment, use gh pr comment NUMBER --body "text" --repo OWNER/REPO.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]    - When adding issue comment, use gh issue comment NUMBER --body "text" --repo OWNER/REPO.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]    - When viewing PR details, use gh pr view NUMBER --repo OWNER/REPO.
[2026-05-01T08:41:40.979Z] [INFO]    - When filtering with jq, use gh api repos/${owner}/${repo}/pulls/${prNumber}/comments --paginate --jq 'reverse | .[0:5]'.

[2026-05-01T08:41:51.241Z] [INFO]         "content": "title:\tPrototype version 0.0.1\nstate:\tOPEN\nauthor:\tkonard (Konstantin Diachenko)\nlabels:\tdocumentation, enhancement\ncomments:\t0\nassignees:\t\nprojects:\t\nmilestone:\t\nnumber:\t1\n--\nPersonal meta profile sovereign system, that is fully local, and respects users privacy and helps to actually own and control his data about his network of contacts, connections and partners.\n\nFeatures:\n- Import/export with unified database and external services (via API and bulk export archive files)\n- Import/export with unified database and .lino files\n- Unified database is stored as both in binary form and text form, for reliability and express export\n- Regular backups of unified database in archive target folder\n- Unified chat UI to support chats in all external services\n- Auto-completion in chat UI based on previous messages from yourself in history\n- Operator UI, that automatically switches between chats/contexts, allowing to focus on efficient manual processing of each unread message based on ideas from https://github.com/link-assistant/operator\n- Patterns editor - allow to automatically infer/constract/simplify/generalize regular expressions and PEGs to match multiple messages. User can use one or multiple messages, and fuzzy search to find candidates from the history, once found user can select to add them pattern to match wider range of messages.\n- Reply message variation group editor, we should use fuzzy search to extract typical replies to the same question or message from the history of own messages, user can add manually as many variations as he wants.\n- Automated scripts, that handle typical messages like hi/hello, thank you, and so on. UI should be similar to n8n, with dependencies between patterns and reply variations. That should visualize typical dialogs tree from history, so that user can configure how to automatically control multi stage conversations, when multiple questions need to be asked once previous answers are gotten. So routing talking, first contact, knowing each other can be automated. It should be run in fully automated more or semi automated mode. In semi automated mode user can see that system detected some condition it can react to, and either manually select reply variation or confirm random selections as it would in fully automated mode.\n- We should be able to use pattern matching on multiple messages, to scan conversations for personal private facts we got to know about each user, to help the user remember all facts where discussed in the each conversation without looking too much at it, saving time. All personal data captured from conversation automatically should be shown in form `question - answer`, for both sides you and your chat partner. For chats with multiple users we should be able to see what questions was answered by each chat participant.\n- CRM features like ability to see in one place what we know about our contacts/friends in one place, ability cross reference, intersect sets of chats, groups, communites, and facts to select target audience and send them all hi/hello or stickers, to start massive manual conversations with only relevant and target audience\n- Ability to search locally for people, communities, companies, messages, chats, with configurable parameters\n- Unified broadcasing UI to support public posting on walls/public posts in all services\n- Profile sync in all services\n- Resumes sync in all services\n- External services support: VK, Telegram, X, WhatsApp, Facebook, Linked-In, career.habr.com, hh.ru, superjob.ru\n- Fully open-source and public domain requiring no trust\n- Every function and feature should be importable as npm library\n- NPM library should contain CLI interface, and electron desktop interface, ability to start web server to connect to local storage\n- All clients should allow sync of the data between them using WebRTC mapped to localstorage or any configurable endpoint, if user wants to host it in the internet to simplify the access from small devices like mobile phones, with ability to work with full local database in personal cloud.\n- Docker microservice for WebRTC server and for web server.\n- We should be able to do universal Links access interface in both server and client. On client like web it should combine access to local cache of data, and direct server access. On server it should combine access to both read and write at the same time to binary storage (link-cli/doublets-rs) and text storage (links notation).\n- We should use JavaScript on server and client and all UI apps with React.js, with Rust + WebAssembly for heavy workloads.\n- We should also have a version of server and microservice fully written in only Rust, but as well in JavaScript.\n- Application should use all the best practices from https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines, https://design.google, and Microsoft design guildelines.\n- The code should be written in simple way with no premature optimizations \n- All code should be documented with automated API documentation generation\n- We should cover all tests fully with unit, integration, and e2e tests using http://github.com/link-foundation/browser-commander\n\nFor all data exchanges and local text data-source (we should prefer indented format to minimize brackets/parentheses):\n- https://github.com/link-foundation/links-notation\n- https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-objects-codec\n\nFor all binary data in mobile, web, desktop, and in CLI/service (if any packages are not http://github.com/linksplatform/doublets-web\n\nFor CLI arguments and configuration:\n- https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-arguments\n\nFor cross-platform apps (iOS, Android, Electron):\n- http://github.com/deep-foundation/sdk\n\nWe should reuse all features from:\n- https://github.com/konard/broadcast\n- https://github.com/konard/vk-bot\n- https://github.com/konard/vk\n- https://github.com/telegramdesktop/tdesktop\n\nAutomated messaging scripts network UI quality as in:\n- https://n8n.io\n\nUse all the best practices from CI/CD templates (check full file tree to [2026-05-01T08:41:56.992Z] [INFO]         "content": "# js-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template\n\nA comprehensive template for AI-driven JavaScript/TypeScript development with full CI/CD pipeline support.\n\n## Features\n\n- **Multi-runtime support**: Works with Bun, Node.js, and Deno\n- **Universal testing**: Uses [test-anywhere](https://github.com/link-foundation/test-anywhere) for cross-runtime tests\n- **Automated releases**: Changesets-based versioning with GitHub Actions\n- **Code quality**: ESLint + Prettier with pre-commit hooks via Husky\n- **Package manager agnostic**: Works with bun, npm, yarn, pnpm, and deno\n- **Broken link checks**: Automated link validation with [lychee](https://github.com/lycheeverse/lychee-action) and Web Archive fallback suggestions\n\n## Quick Start\n\n### Using This Template\n\n1. Click \"Use this template\" on GitHub to create a new repository\n2. Clone your new repository\n3. Update `package.json` with your package name and description\n4. Update the `PACKAGE_NAME` constant in these scripts:\n   - `scripts/validate-changeset.mjs`\n   - `scripts/merge-changesets.mjs`\n   - `scripts/publish-to-npm.mjs`\n   - `scripts/format-release-notes.mjs`\n   - `scripts/create-manual-changeset.mjs`\n5. Install dependencies: `bun install`\n6. Start developing!\n\n### Development\n\n```bash\n# Install dependencies\nbun install\n\n# Run tests\nbun test\n\n# Or with other runtimes:\nnpm test\ndeno test --allow-read\n\n# Lint code\nbun run lint\n\n# Format code\nbun run format\n\n# Check all (lint + format + file size)\nbun run check\n```\n\n## Project Structure\n\n```\n.\n├── .changeset/           # Changeset configuration\n├── .github/workflows/    # GitHub Actions CI/CD\n├── .husky/               # Git hooks (pre-commit)\n├── examples/             # Usage examples\n├── scripts/              # Build and release scripts\n├── src/                  # Source code\n│   ├── index.js          # Main entry point\n│   └── index.d.ts        # TypeScript definitions\n├── tests/                # Test files\n├── .eslintrc.js          # ESLint configuration\n├── .prettierrc           # Prettier configuration\n├── bunfig.toml           # Bun configuration\n├── deno.json             # Deno configuration\n└── package.json          # Node.js package manifest\n```\n\n## Design Choices\n\n### Multi-Runtime Support\n\nThis template is designed to work seamlessly with all major JavaScript runtimes:\n\n- **Bun**: Primary runtime with highest performance, uses native test support (`bun test`)\n- **Node.js**: Alternative runtime, uses built-in test runner (`node --test`)\n- **Deno**: Secure runtime with built-in TypeScript support (`deno test`)\n\nThe [test-anywhere](https://github.com/link-foundation/test-anywhere) framework provides a unified testing API that works identically across all runtimes.\n\n### Package Manager Agnostic\n\nWhile `package.json` is the source of truth for dependencies, the template supports:\n\n- **bun**: Primary choice, uses `bun.lockb`\n- **npm**: Uses `package-lock.json`\n- **yarn**: Uses `yarn.lock`\n- **pnpm**: Uses `pnpm-lock.yaml`\n- **deno**: Uses `deno.json` for configuration\n\nNote: `package-lock.json` is not committed by default to allow any package manager.\n\n### Code Quality\n\n- **ESLint**: Configured with recommended rules + Prettier integration\n- **Prettier**: Consistent code formatting\n- **Husky + lint-staged**: Pre-commit hooks ensure code quality\n- **File size limit**: Files must stay under 1500 lines for maintainability (enforced via ESLint and CI)\n\n### Release Workflow\n\nThe release workflow uses [Changesets](https://github.com/changesets/changesets) for version management:\n\n1. **Creating a changeset**: Run `bun run changeset` to document changes\n2. **PR validation**: CI checks for valid changeset in each PR\n3. **Automated versioning**: Merging to `main` triggers version bump\n4. **npm publishing**: Automated via OIDC trusted publishing (no tokens needed)\n5. **GitHub releases**: Auto-created with formatted release notes\n\n#### Manual Releases\n\nTwo manual release modes are available via GitHub Actions:\n\n- **Instant release**: Immediately bump version and publish\n- **Changeset PR**: Create a PR with changeset for review\n\n### CI/CD Pipeline\n\nThe GitHub Actions workflow (`.github/workflows/release.yml`) implements a fast-fail pipeline:\n\n**Fast checks** (~7-30s each, run first for fastest feedback):\n\n1. **Test compilation**: Syntax-checks all `.mjs` files with `node --check`\n2. **Lint, format & secrets scan**: ESLint, Prettier, jscpd, and [secretlint](https://github.com/secretlint/secretlint) for credential leak detection\n3. **File line limits**: Enforces 1500-line limit on `.mjs` files and `release.yml`\n4. **Changeset check**: Validates PR has exactly one changeset (added by that PR)\n5. **Version check**: Blocks manual version changes in `package.json`\n6. **Documentation validation**: Checks doc file sizes and required files\n\n**Slow checks** (only run after all fast checks pass):\n\n7. **Test matrix**: 3 runtimes × 3 OS = 9 test combinations\n8. **Broken link checks**: Validates all links in Markdown/HTML files (separate workflow)\n\n**Release** (on merge to main):\n\n9. **Changeset merge**: Combines multiple pending changesets at release time\n10. **Release**: Automated versioning and npm publishing\n\nSee [BEST-PRACTICES.md](docs/BEST-PRACTICES.md) for detailed explanations of each practice.\n\n#### Robust Changeset Handling\n\nThe CI/CD pipeline is designed to handle concurrent PRs gracefully:\n\n- **PR Validation**: Only validates changesets **added by the current PR**, not pre-existing ones from other merged PRs. This prevents false failures when multiple PRs merge before a release cycle completes.\n\n- **Release-time Merging**: If multiple changesets exist when releasing, they are automatically merged into a single changeset with:\n  - The highest version bump type (major > minor > patch)\n  - All descriptions preserved in chronological order\n\nThis design decouples PR validation from the need to pull changes from the default branch, reducing conflicts and ensuring that even if CI/CD fails, all unpublished changesets will still get published when the error is resolved.\n\n### Broken Link Checker\n\nThe link checker workflow (`.github/workflows/links.yml`) validates all links in Markdown and HTML files:\n\n1. **Detection**: Uses [lychee](https://github.com/lycheeverse/lychee-action) to scan all `*.md` and `*.html` files\n2. **Web Archive fallback**: For any broken links found, automatically checks the [Wayback Machine](https://web.archive.org) for archived versions\n3. **Actionable suggestions**: Reports one of three outcomes for each broken link:\n   - **Archived**: Suggests the Web Archive URL as a replacement\n   - **Not archived**: Clearly reports the link is unrecoverable\n4. **Scheduled checks**: Runs weekly to catch links that break over time (even if no files changed)\n5. **Issue creation**: On scheduled runs, creates a GitHub Issue with the full broken links report\n\nAdd regex patterns to `.lycheeignore` to exclude URLs from checks (e.g., local dev URLs, example.com, known rate-limited sites).\n\n## Configuration\n\n### Updating Package Name\n\nAfter creating a repository from this template, update the package name in:\n\n1. `package.json`: `\"name\": \"your-package-name\"`\n2. `.changeset/config.json`: Package references\n3. Scripts that reference the package name (see Quick Start)\n\n### ESLint Rules\n\nCustomize ESLint in `eslint.config.js`. Current configuration:\n\n- ES Modules support\n- Prettier integration\n- No console restrictions (common in CLI tools)\n- Strict equality enforcement\n- Async/await best practices\n- **Strict unused variables rule**: No exceptions - all unused variables, arguments, and caught errors must be removed (no `_` prefix exceptions)\n\n### Prettier Options\n\nConfigured in `.prettierrc`:\n\n- Single quotes\n- Semicolons\n- 2-space indentation\n- 80-character line width\n- ES5 trailing commas\n- LF line endings\n\n## Scripts Reference\n\n| Script                 | Description                             |\n| ---------------------- | --------------------------------------- |\n| `bun test`             | Run tests with Bun                      |\n| `bun run lint`         | Check code with ESLint                  |\n| `bun run lint:fix`     | Fix ESLint issues automatically         |\n| `bun run format`       | Format code with Prettier               |\n| `bun run format:check` | Check formatting without changing files |\n| `bun run check`        | Run all checks (lint + format)          |\n| `bun run changeset`    | Create a new changeset                  |\n\n## Contributing\n\nSee [CONTRIBUTING.md](docs/CONTRIBUTING.md) for detailed contribution guidelines.\n\nQuick steps:\n\n1. Fork the repository\n2. Create a feature branch: `git checkout -b feature/my-feature`\n3. Make your changes\n4. Create a changeset: `bun run changeset`\n5. Commit your changes (pre-commit hooks will run automatically)\n6. Push and create a Pull Request\n\n## Best Practices\n\nThis template implements CI/CD best practices for AI-driven development. See [BEST-PRACTICES.md](docs/BEST-PRACTICES.md) for details on:\n\n- File size limits for AI readability\n- Automated formatting and linting\n- Multi-runtime and cross-platform testing\n- Changeset-based versioning\n- Concurrency control for CI/CD pipelines\n\n## License\n\n[Unlicense](LICENSE) - Public Domain",

Install dependencies: `bun install`\n6. Start developing!\n\n### Development\n\n```bash\n# Install dependencies\nbun install\n\n# Run tests\nbun test\n\n# Or with other runtimes:\nnpm test\ndeno test --allow-read\n\n# Lint code\nbun run lint\n\n# Format code\nbun run format\n\n# Check all (lint + format + file size)\nbun run check\n```\n\n## Project Structure\n\n```\n.\n├── .changeset/           # Changeset configuration\n├── .github/workflows/    # GitHub Actions CI/CD\n├── .husky/               # Git hooks (pre-commit)\n├── examples/             # Usage examples\n├── scripts/              # Build and release scripts\n├── src/                  # Source code\n│   ├── index.js          # Main entry point\n│   └── index.d.ts        # TypeScript definitions\n├── tests/                # Test files\n├── .eslintrc.js          # ESLint configuration\n├── .prettierrc           # Prettier configuration\n├── bunfig.toml           # Bun configuration\n├── deno.json             # Deno configuration\n└── package.json          # Node.js package manifest\n```\n\n## Design Choices\n\n### Multi-Runtime Support\n\nThis template is designed to work seamlessly with all major JavaScript runtimes:\n\n- **Bun**: Primary runtime with highest performance, uses native test support (`bun test`)\n- **Node.js**: Alternative runtime, uses built-in test runner (`node --test`)\n- **Deno**: Secure runtime with built-in TypeScript support (`deno test`)\n\nThe [test-anywhere](https://github.com/link-foundation/test-anywhere) framework provides a unified testing API that works identically across all runtimes.\n\n### Package Manager Agnostic\n\nWhile `package.json` is the source of truth for dependencies, the template supports:\n\n- **bun**: Primary choice, uses `bun.lockb`\n- **npm**: Uses `package-lock.json`\n- **yarn**: Uses `yarn.lock`\n- **pnpm**: Uses `pnpm-lock.yaml`\n- **deno**: Uses `deno.json` for configuration\n\nNote: `package-lock.json` is not committed by default to allow any package manager.\n\n### Code Quality\n\n- **ESLint**: Configured with recommended rules + Prettier integration\n- **Prettier**: Consistent code formatting\n- **Husky + lint-staged**: Pre-commit hooks ensure code quality\n- **File size limit**: Files must stay under 1500 lines for maintainability (enforced via ESLint and CI)\n\n### Release Workflow\n\nThe release workflow uses [Changesets](https://github.com/changesets/changesets) for version management:\n\n1. **Creating a changeset**: Run `bun run changeset` to document changes\n2. **PR validation**: CI checks for valid changeset in each PR\n3. **Automated versioning**: Merging to `main` triggers version bump\n4. **npm publishing**: Automated via OIDC trusted publishing (no tokens needed)\n5. **GitHub releases**: Auto-created with formatted release notes\n\n#### Manual Releases\n\nTwo manual release modes are available via GitHub Actions:\n\n- **Instant release**: Immediately bump version and publish\n- **Changeset PR**: Create a PR with changeset for review\n\n### CI/CD Pipeline\n\nThe GitHub Actions workflow (`.github/workflows/release.yml`) implements a fast-fail pipeline:\n\n**Fast checks** (~7-30s each, run first for fastest feedback):\n\n1. **Test compilation**: Syntax-checks all `.mjs` files with `node --check`\n2. **Lint, format & secrets scan**: ESLint, Prettier, jscpd, and [secretlint](https://github.com/secretlint/secretlint) for credential leak detection\n3. **File line limits**: Enforces 1500-line limit on `.mjs` files and `release.yml`\n4. **Changeset check**: Validates PR has exactly one changeset (added by that PR)\n5. **Version check**: Blocks manual version changes in `package.json`\n6. **Documentation validation**: Checks doc file sizes and required files\n\n**Slow checks** (only run after all fast checks pass):\n\n7. **Test matrix**: 3 runtimes × 3 OS = 9 test combinations\n8. **Broken link checks**: Validates all links in Markdown/HTML files (separate workflow)\n\n**Release** (on merge to main):\n\n9. **Changeset merge**: Combines multiple pending changesets at release time\n10. **Release**: Automated versioning and npm publishing\n\nSee [BEST-PRACTICES.md](docs/BEST-PRACTICES.md) for detailed explanations of each practice.\n\n#### Robust Changeset Handling\n\nThe CI/CD pipeline is designed to handle concurrent PRs gracefully:\n\n- **PR Validation**: Only validates changesets **added by the current PR**, not pre-existing ones from other merged PRs. This prevents false failures when multiple PRs merge before a release cycle completes.\n\n- **Release-time Merging**: If multiple changesets exist when releasing, they are automatically merged into a single changeset with:\n  - The highest version bump type (major > minor > patch)\n  - All descriptions preserved in chronological order\n\nThis design decouples PR validation from the need to pull changes from the default branch, reducing conflicts and ensuring that even if CI/CD fails, all unpublished changesets will still get published when the error is resolved.\n\n### Broken Link Checker\n\nThe link checker workflow (`.github/workflows/links.yml`) validates all links in Markdown and HTML files:\n\n1. **Detection**: Uses [lychee](https://github.com/lycheeverse/lychee-action) to scan all `*.md` and `*.html` files\n2. **Web Archive fallback**: For any broken links found, automatically checks the [Wayback Machine](https://web.archive.org) for archived versions\n3. **Actionable suggestions**: Reports one of three outcomes for each broken link:\n   - **Archived**: Suggests the Web Archive URL as a replacement\n   - **Not archived**: Clearly reports the link is unrecoverable\n4. **Scheduled checks**: Runs weekly to catch links that break over time (even if no files changed)\n5. **Issue creation**: On scheduled runs, creates a GitHub Issue with the full broken links report\n\nAdd regex patterns to `.lycheeignore` to exclude URLs from checks (e.g., local dev URLs, example.com, known rate-limited sites).\n\n## Configuration\n\n### Updating Package Name\n\nAfter creating a repository from this template, update the package name in:\n\n1. `package.json`: `\"name\": \"your-package-name\"`\n2. `.changeset/config.json`: Package references\n3. Scripts that reference the package name (see Quick Start)\n\n### ESLint Rules\n\nCustomize ESLint in `eslint.config.js`. Current configuration:\n\n- ES Modules support\n- Prettier integration\n- No console restrictions (common in CLI tools)\n- Strict equality enforcement\n- Async/await best practices\n- **Strict unused variables rule**: No exceptions - all unused variables, arguments, and caught errors must be removed (no `_` prefix exceptions)\n\n### Prettier Options\n\nConfigured in `.prettierrc`:\n\n- Single quotes\n- Semicolons\n- 2-space indentation\n- 80-character line width\n- ES5 trailing commas\n- LF line endings\n\n## Scripts Reference\n\n| Script                 | Description                             |\n| ---------------------- | --------------------------------------- |\n| `bun test`             | Run tests with Bun                      |\n| `bun run lint`         | Check code with ESLint                  |\n| `bun run lint:fix`     | Fix ESLint issues automatically         |\n| `bun run format`       | Format code with Prettier               |\n| `bun run format:check` | Check formatting without changing files |\n| `bun run check`        | Run all checks (lint + format)          |\n| `bun run changeset`    | Create a new changeset                  |\n\n## Contributing\n\nSee [CONTRIBUTING.md](docs/CONTRIBUTING.md) for detailed contribution guidelines.\n\nQuick steps:\n\n1. Fork the repository\n2. Create a feature branch: `git checkout -b feature/my-feature`\n3. Make your changes\n4. Create a changeset: `bun run changeset`\n5. Commit your changes (pre-commit hooks will run automatically)\n6. Push and create a Pull Request\n\n## Best Practices\n\nThis template implements CI/CD best practices for AI-driven development. See [BEST-PRACTICES.md](docs/BEST-PRACTICES.md) for details on:\n\n- File size limits for AI readability\n- Automated formatting and linting\n- Multi-runtime and cross-platform testing\n- Changeset-based versioning\n- Concurrency control for CI/CD pipelines\n\n## License\n\n[Unlicense](LICENSE) - Public Domain",

[2026-05-01T08:42:21.608Z] [INFO]         "type": "tool_result",
[2026-05-01T08:42:21.608Z] [INFO]         "content": "# Case Study: Issue #23 - Best CI/CD Practices from Hive Mind Repository\n\n## Summary\n\nThis case study documents the incorporation of best CI/CD practices from the [hive-mind repository](https://github.com/link-assistant/hive-mind), specifically addressing the critical issue of **stale merge preview** in GitHub Actions. The key insight comes from [hive-mind issue #1141](https://github.com/link-assistant/hive-mind/issues/1141) and its fix in [PR #1142](https://github.com/link-assistant/hive-mind/pull/1142).\n\n## The Problem: GitHub's Stale Merge Preview Architecture\n\n### How GitHub Actions Handles Pull Requests\n\nWhen a PR triggers a GitHub Actions workflow (`on: pull_request`), GitHub performs these steps:\n\n1. Creates a synthetic merge commit at `refs/pull/{number}/merge`\n2. This merge commit represents what the merge **would look like** at the moment of creation\n3. `actions/checkout@v4` checks out this merge preview by default\n4. **The merge preview does NOT automatically update** when the base branch changes\n\n### The Critical Gap\n\n```\nDay 1: PR opened/synced\n       -> GitHub creates merge preview (validates correctly)\n       -> CI runs on merge preview -> PASSES\n\n[Time passes - other PRs merge to main, changing the codebase]\n\nDay N: PR merged (without re-running CI)\n       -> Actual merge result differs from stale preview\n       -> Push CI runs on actual merge -> FAILS\n```\n\nThis is exactly what happened in hive-mind issue #1141, where:\n\n- Jan 11: PR #1105 CI passed (merge preview showed file at 1495 lines)\n- Jan 19: PR merged (actual result was 1506 lines due to intervening changes)\n- Push CI failed because the actual merge exceeded the 1500-line limit\n\n### Why This Matters\n\nThe stale merge preview issue creates a **false sense of security**:\n\n- Developers see green CI checks on their PR\n- They merge confidently\n- Main branch CI fails after merge\n- The team scrambles to fix a broken main branch\n\n## Solutions Implemented\n\n### 1. Fresh Merge Simulation in CI Workflow\n\nAdded to both `lint` and `test` jobs in `.github/workflows/release.yml`:\n\n```yaml\n- name: Simulate fresh merge with base branch (PR only)\n  if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'\n  env:\n    BASE_REF: ${{ github.base_ref }}\n  run: |\n    # Fetch the latest base branch\n    git fetch origin \"$BASE_REF\"\n\n    # Check if base branch has new commits\n    BEHIND_COUNT=$(git rev-list --count HEAD..origin/$BASE_REF)\n\n    if [ \"$BEHIND_COUNT\" -gt 0 ]; then\n      # Merge latest base branch to simulate actual merge result\n      git merge origin/$BASE_REF --no-edit || exit 1\n    fi\n```\n\nThis ensures PR CI validates the **actual** merge result, not a stale snapshot.\n\n### 2. ESLint max-lines Rule\n\nAlready configured in `eslint.config.js`:\n\n```javascript\n'max-lines': ['error', 1500]\n```\n\nThis provides:\n\n- Local development feedback via editor integration\n- CI enforcement via `npm run lint`\n- Early warning before files become unmaintainable\n\n### 3. Version Check CI Job\n\nAlready configured in `.github/workflows/release.yml`:\n\n```yaml\nversion-check:\n  name: Check for Manual Version Changes\n  runs-on: ubuntu-latest\n  if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'\n  steps:\n    - uses: actions/checkout@v4\n      with:\n        fetch-depth: 0\n    - name: Check for version changes in package.json",

[2026-05-01T08:42:21.608Z] [INFO]     "stdout": "# Case Study: Issue #23 - Best CI/CD Practices from Hive Mind Repository\n\n## Summary\n\nThis case study documents the incorporation of best CI/CD practices from the [hive-mind repository](https://github.com/link-assistant/hive-mind), specifically addressing the critical issue of **stale merge preview** in GitHub Actions. The key insight comes from [hive-mind issue #1141](https://github.com/link-assistant/hive-mind/issues/1141) and its fix in [PR #1142](https://github.com/link-assistant/hive-mind/pull/1142).\n\n## The Problem: GitHub's Stale Merge Preview Architecture\n\n### How GitHub Actions Handles Pull Requests\n\nWhen a PR triggers a GitHub Actions workflow (`on: pull_request`), GitHub performs these steps:\n\n1. Creates a synthetic merge commit at `refs/pull/{number}/merge`\n2. This merge commit represents what the merge **would look like** at the moment of creation\n3. `actions/checkout@v4` checks out this merge preview by default\n4. **The merge preview does NOT automatically update** when the base branch changes\n\n### The Critical Gap\n\n```\nDay 1: PR opened/synced\n       -> GitHub creates merge preview (validates correctly)\n       -> CI runs on merge preview -> PASSES\n\n[Time passes - other PRs merge to main, changing the codebase]\n\nDay N: PR merged (without re-running CI)\n       -> Actual merge result differs from stale preview\n       -> Push CI runs on actual merge -> FAILS\n```\n\nThis is exactly what happened in hive-mind issue #1141, where:\n\n- Jan 11: PR #1105 CI passed (merge preview showed file at 1495 lines)\n- Jan 19: PR merged (actual result was 1506 lines due to intervening changes)\n- Push CI failed because the actual merge exceeded the 1500-line limit\n\n### Why This Matters\n\nThe stale merge preview issue creates a **false sense of security**:\n\n- Developers see green CI checks on their PR\n- They merge confidently\n- Main branch CI fails after merge\n- The team scrambles to fix a broken main branch\n\n## Solutions Implemented\n\n### 1. Fresh Merge Simulation in CI Workflow\n\nAdded to both `lint` and `test` jobs in `.github/workflows/release.yml`:\n\n```yaml\n- name: Simulate fresh merge with base branch (PR only)\n  if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'\n  env:\n    BASE_REF: ${{ github.base_ref }}\n  run: |\n    # Fetch the latest base branch\n    git fetch origin \"$BASE_REF\"\n\n    # Check if base branch has new commits\n    BEHIND_COUNT=$(git rev-list --count HEAD..origin/$BASE_REF)\n\n    if [ \"$BEHIND_COUNT\" -gt 0 ]; then\n      # Merge latest base branch to simulate actual merge result\n      git merge origin/$BASE_REF --no-edit || exit 1\n    fi\n```\n\nThis ensures PR CI validates the **actual** merge result, not a stale snapshot.\n\n### 2. ESLint max-lines Rule\n\nAlready configured in `eslint.config.js`:\n\n```javascript\n'max-lines': ['error', 1500]\n```\n\nThis provides:\n\n- Local development feedback via editor integration\n- CI enforcement via `npm run lint`\n- Early warning before files become unmaintainable\n\n### 3. [2026-05-01T08:42:25.928Z] [INFO]         "content": "# Case Study: CI/CD Best Practices Alignment with hive-mind\n\n**Issue:** [#29](https://github.com/link-foundation/js-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template/issues/29)\n**Source:** [link-assistant/hive-mind](https://github.com/link-assistant/hive-mind) CI/CD best practices\n\n## Problem Statement\n\nThe JS AI-driven development pipeline template should incorporate all proven CI/CD best practices from the hive-mind repository. A comprehensive comparison revealed several gaps between the two repositories' CI/CD configurations.\n\n## Gap Analysis\n\n### Practices Already Implemented\n\n| Practice                                              | Status      | Notes                                    |\n| ----------------------------------------------------- | ----------- | ---------------------------------------- |\n| Change detection (`detect-changes` job)               | Implemented | Gates jobs on relevant file changes      |\n| File size limits (ESLint `max-lines: 1500`)           | Implemented | ESLint rule enforced                     |\n| Automated code formatting (ESLint + Prettier + Husky) | Implemented | Pre-commit hooks active                  |\n| Static analysis & linting                             | Implemented | ESLint with strict rules                 |\n| Changeset-based versioning                            | Implemented | @changesets/cli with validation          |\n| Pre-commit hooks                                      | Implemented | Husky + lint-staged                      |\n| Release automation (OIDC trusted publishing)          | Implemented | npm OIDC + dual trigger modes            |\n| Concurrency control                                   | Implemented | Cancel-in-progress on main, queue on PRs |\n| Fresh merge simulation                                | Implemented | Inline in lint and test jobs             |\n| Version change prohibition                            | Implemented | check-version.mjs blocks manual edits    |\n| Broken link checking                                  | Implemented | lychee + Web Archive fallback            |\n| Cross-runtime testing (Node.js, Bun, Deno)            | Implemented | 3x3 matrix                               |\n| Cross-platform testing (Ubuntu, macOS, Windows)       | Implemented | 3x3 matrix                               |\n| Code duplication detection (jscpd)                    | Implemented | Threshold-based checking                 |\n\n### Gaps Identified\n\n| #   | Gap                                       | hive-mind Practice                                                         | Impact                                                            |\n| --- | ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- |\n| 1   | No test-compilation job                   | Fast syntax check (`node --check` on all `.mjs` files) runs in ~7s         | Missing fast-fail gate for syntax errors                          |\n| 2   | No file line limits CI check              | Separate `check-file-line-limits.sh` checks `.mjs` files AND `release.yml` | ESLint only checks `src/` files; scripts and workflow not checked |\n| 3   | No fast-fail job ordering                 | Fast checks gate slow checks to give fastest feedback                      | Slow test matrix runs even when lint fails                        |\n| 4   | Uses `always()` instead of `!cancelled()` | Issue #1278: `always()` prevents cancellation propagation                  | Cancelled runs still execute dependent jobs                       |\n| 5   | No secrets detection                      | Principle #11: Scan for accidental credential leaks                        | Security risk: secrets could be committed                         |\n| 6   | No documentation validation               | Principle #12: Validate docs like code                                     | Docs can drift from actual behavior                               |\n| 7   | Inline fresh merge simulation             | hive-mind extracts to `scripts/simulate-fresh-merge.sh`                    | Code duplication across lint and test jobs                        |\n\n## Solutions Implemented\n\n### 1. Test Compilation Job (Fast Syntax Check)\n\nAdded `scripts/check-mjs-syntax.sh` and a `test-compilation` job that runs `node --check` on all `.mjs` files. This catches syntax errors in ~7 seconds, before the full test suite runs.\n\n### 2. File Line Limits Check\n\nAdded `scripts/check-file-line-limits.sh` and a `check-file-line-limits` job. This enforces the 1500-line limit on `.mjs` files and `release.yml`, complementing the ESLint rule which only covers `src/` files.\n\n### 3. Fast-Fail Job Ordering\n\nRestructured job dependencies so slow checks (test matrix) only run after fast checks (test-compilation, lint, check-file-line-limits) pass. This gives the fastest possible feedback loop for AI solvers.\n\n### 4. Fixed `always()` → `!cancelled()`\n\nReplaced `always()` with `!cancelled()` in test job conditions per hive-mind issue #1278. This ensures that when a workflow run is cancelled, dependent jobs also cancel instead of continuing to run.\n\n### 5. Secrets Detection\n\nAdded a `secretlint` step in the lint job using `@secretlint/secretlint-rule-preset-recommend`. This catches accidental credential leaks before code reaches review.\n\n### 6. Documentation Validation\n\nAdded a `validate-docs` job that runs when docs change, checking for file size limits and structural requirements in documentation files.\n\n### 7. Extracted Fresh Merge Simulation\n\nMoved the duplicated inline fresh merge simulation to `scripts/simulate-fresh-merge.sh`, reducing duplication across the lint and test jobs.\n\n## References\n\n- [hive-mind CI/CD Best Practices](https://github.com/link-assistant/hive-mind/blob/main/docs/CI-CD-BEST-PRACTICES.md)\n- [hive-mind release.yml](https://github.com/link-assistant/hive-mind/blob/main/.github/workflows/release.yml) (~1290 lines)\n- [Issue #1274](https://github.com/link-assistant/hive-mind/issues/1274) - Concurrency blocking\n- [Issue #1278](https://github.com/link-assistant/hive-mind/issues/1278) - `always()` cancellation prevention\n- [Issue #1141](https://github.com/link-assistant/hive-mind/issues/1141) - Stale merge preview",

Foundation's Links Notation Class Library.\n\n![introduction](./docs/comparison/comparison.svg \"json, xml and lino comparison\")\n\nThis library gives you an ability to convert any string that contains\nlinks notation into a list of links and back to the string after\nmodifications are made.\n\nLinks notation is based on two concepts references and links. Each\nreference references other link. The notation supports links with any\nnumber of references to other links.\n\n## Quick Start\n\n### C&#35;\n\n```csharp\nvar parser = new Link.Foundation.Links.Notation.Parser();\nvar links = parser.Parse(\"papa (lovesMama: loves mama)\");\n```\n\n### JavaScript\n\n```javascript\nimport { Parser } from 'links-notation';\nconst parser = new Parser();\nconst links = parser.parse(\"papa (lovesMama: loves mama)\");\n```\n\n### Rust\n\n```rust\nuse links_notation::parse_lino;\nlet links = parse_lino(\"papa (lovesMama: loves mama)\").unwrap();\n```\n\n### Python\n\n```python\nfrom links_notation import Parser\nparser = Parser()\nlinks = parser.parse(\"papa (lovesMama: loves mama)\")\n```\n\n### Go\n\n```go\nimport lino \"github.com/link-foundation/links-notation/go\"\nlinks, _ := lino.Parse(\"papa (lovesMama: loves mama)\")\n```\n\n### Java\n\n```java\nimport io.github.linkfoundation.linksnotation.Parser;\nParser parser = new Parser();\nList<Link> links = parser.parse(\"papa (lovesMama: loves mama)\");\n```\n\n## Examples",

[2026-05-01T08:42:53.060Z] [INFO]         "content": "name:\tlink-foundation/lino-objects-codec\ndescription:\tA library to encode/decode objects to/from links notation\n--\n# lino-objects-codec\n\n[![JS CI](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-objects-codec/actions/workflows/js.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-objects-codec/actions/workflows/js.yml)\n[![Python CI](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-objects-codec/actions/workflows/python.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-objects-codec/actions/workflows/python.yml)\n[![Rust CI](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-objects-codec/actions/workflows/rust.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-objects-codec/actions/workflows/rust.yml)\n[![C# CI](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-objects-codec/actions/workflows/csharp.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-objects-codec/actions/workflows/csharp.yml)\n[![Python Version](https://img.shields.io/pypi/pyversions/lino-objects-codec.svg)](https://pypi.org/project/lino-objects-codec/)\n\nUniversal serialization library to encode/decode objects to/from Links Notation format. Available in **Python**, **JavaScript**, **Rust**, and **C#** with identical functionality and API design.\n\n## 🌍 Multi-Language Support\n\nThis library provides universal serialization and deserialization with built-in support for circular references and complex object graphs in:\n\n- **[Python](python/)** - Full implementation for Python 3.8+\n- **[JavaScript](js/)** - Full implementation for Node.js 18+\n- **[Rust](rust/)** - Full implementation for Rust 1.70+\n- **[C#](csharp/)** - Full implementation for .NET 8.0+\n\nAll implementations share the same design philosophy and provide feature parity.\n\n## Features\n\n- **Universal Serialization**: Encode objects to Links Notation format\n- **Type Support**: Handle all common types in each language:\n  - **Python**: `None`, `bool`, `int`, `float`, `str`, `list`, `dict`\n  - **JavaScript**: `null`, `undefined`, `boolean`, `number`, `string`, `Array`, `Object`\n  - **Rust**: `LinoValue` enum with `Null`, `Bool`, `Int`, `Float`, `String`, `Array`, `Object`\n  - **C#**: `null`, `bool`, `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `string`, `List<object?>`, `Dictionary<string, object?>`\n  - Special float/number values: `NaN`, `Infinity`, `-Infinity`\n- **Circular References**: Automatically detect and preserve circular references\n- **Object Identity**: Maintain object identity for shared references\n- **UTF-8 Support**: Full Unicode string support using base64 encoding\n- **Simple API**: Easy-to-use `encode()` and `decode()` functions\n- **JSON/Lino Conversion**: Convert between JSON and Links Notation (JavaScript)\n- **Reference Escaping**: Properly escape strings for Links Notation format (JavaScript)\n- **Fuzzy Matching**: String similarity utilities for finding matches (JavaScript)\n- **Indented Format**: Human-readable indented Links Notation format for display and debugging\n\n## Quick Start\n\n### Python\n\n```bash\npip install lino-objects-codec\n```\n\n```python\nfrom link_notation_objects_codec import encode, decode\n\n# Encode and decode\ndata = {\"name\": \"Alice\", \"age\": 30, \"active\": True}\nencoded = encode(data)\ndecoded = decode(encoded)\nassert decoded == data\n```\n\n### JavaScript\n\n```bash\nnpm install lino-objects-codec\n```\n\n```javascript\nimport { encode, decode } from 'lino-objects-codec';\n\n// Encode and decode\nconst data = { name: 'Alice', age: 30, active: true };\nconst encoded = encode(data);\nconst decoded = decode(encoded);\nconsole.log(JSON.stringify(decoded) === JSON.stringify(data)); // true\n```\n\n### Rust\n\n```toml\n[dependencies]",

[2026-05-01T08:42:54.238Z] [INFO]         "content": "name:\tlink-foundation/link-cli\ndescription:\tA CLI tool to manipulate links.\n--\n# link-cli\n`clink` (`CLInk` `cLINK`), a CLI tool to manipulate links using single substitution operation.\n\nIt is based on [associative theory](https://habr.com/ru/articles/895896) (also in [ru](https://habr.com/ru/articles/804617)) and [Links Notation](https://github.com/linksplatform/Protocols.Lino) (also in [ru](https://github.com/linksplatform/Protocols.Lino/blob/main/README.ru.md))\n\nIt uses C# implementation of [a links data store](https://github.com/linksplatform?view_as=public) (see also in [ru](https://github.com/linksplatform/.github/blob/main/profile/README.ru.md)).\n\nThis CLI tool can be installed globally as `clink` using single command (that will work if you have [.NET](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/en-us/download) installed):\n\n```bash\ndotnet tool install --global clink\n```\n\n<img width=\"811\" alt=\"Screenshot 2025-05-16 at 5 48 06 AM\" src=\"https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/615df4ce-658e-4bab-a483-96fae200f106\" />\n\n\nThis tool provides all CRUD operations for links using single [substitution operation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Markov_algorithm) ([ru](https://ru.wikipedia.org/wiki/Нормальный_алгоритм)) which is turing complete.\n\nEach operations split into two parts:\n\n```\n(matching pattern)\n(substitution pattern)\n```\n\nWhen match pattern and substitution pattern are essensially the same we get no changes (no operation), it may seem like it does not any write, but it actually does the read operation.\n\nFor example when `--changes` option is enabled this operation:\n\n```\n((1: 1 1)) ((1: 1 1))\n```\n\nwill output:\n\n```\n((1: 1 1)) ((1: 1 1))\n```\n\nThat is change of 1-st link with start (source) at itself and end (target) at itself to itself. Meaning no change, but as match pattern applies only to the link with 1 as index, 1 as source and 1 as target, this \"no change\" can be used as read query.\n\nCreation is just a replacement of nothing to something:\n\n```\n() ((1 1))\n```\n\nWhere first `()` is just empty sequence of links, that symbolizes nothing. And `((1 1))` is a sequence of link with 1 as a start and 1 as end, the index is undefined so it for database to decide actual available id (index).\n\nDeletion is just a replacement of something to nothing:\n\n```\n((1 1)) () \n```\n\nWhere `((1 1))` is a sequence of match patterns, with a single pattern for a link with 1 as a start and 1 as end, the index is undefined, meaning it can be any index. It will match only existing link, if no such link found there will be no match. Last `()` is just empty sequence of links, that symbolizes nothing. We don't have matched link on the right side, meaning it will be effectively deleted.\n\nAnd the update is substitution itself, obviously.\n\n```\n((1: 1 1)) ((1: 1 2))\n```\n\nIn that case we have a link with 1-st id on both sides, meaning it is not deleted and not created, it is changed. In this particular example with change the target of the link (its ending) to 2. 2 is ofcourse id of another link. In here we have only links, nothing else.\n\n## Create single link\n\nCreate link with 1 as source and 1 as target.\n\n```bash\nclink '() ((1 1))' --changes --after\n```\n→\n```\n() ((1: 1 1))\n(1: 1 1)\n```",

[2026-05-01T08:42:55.228Z] [INFO]         "content": "name:\tlinksplatform/doublets-rs\ndescription:\t\n--\n# Doublets\n\n[![CI/CD Pipeline](https://github.com/linksplatform/doublets-rs/actions/workflows/release.yml/badge.svg?branch=main)](https://github.com/linksplatform/doublets-rs/actions/workflows/release.yml?query=branch%3Amain)\n[![Crates.io](https://img.shields.io/crates/v/doublets.svg)](https://crates.io/crates/doublets)\n[![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-Unlicense-blue.svg)](https://github.com/linksplatform/doublets-rs/blob/main/LICENSE)\n\nLinksPlatform's Rust implementation of Doublets (associative storage links).\n\nRust port of [Data.Doublets](https://github.com/linksplatform/Data.Doublets) library.\n\n## Overview\n\nDoublets is an associative data structure that represents a link store where each link consists of:\n- **Index** - unique identifier of the link\n- **Source** - the link's source (can reference itself or another link)\n- **Target** - the link's target (can reference itself or another link)\n\nThis simple yet powerful structure can represent any data model, from traditional key-value pairs to complex graph structures. Doublets provide a unified approach to data storage with constant-time lookup operations using size-balanced trees.\n\n### Key Features\n\n- **File-mapped storage** - data persists directly to disk using memory-mapped files\n- **Generic link types** - supports any unsigned integer type (`u8`, `u16`, `u32`, `u64`, `usize`)\n- **Two storage modes**:\n  - `unit::Store` - combined data and index storage in a single memory region\n  - `split::Store` - separated data and index storage for optimized memory layouts\n- **Thread-safe** - `Send` and `Sync` implementations for concurrent access\n- **Query system** - flexible pattern matching using `any` constants\n- **FFI bindings** - C-compatible foreign function interface for cross-language integration\n\n## Installation\n\nAdd to your `Cargo.toml`:\n\n```toml\n[dependencies]\ndoublets = \"0.1.0-pre\"\n```\n\n**Note:** This crate requires nightly Rust due to usage of experimental features.\n\n```bash\nrustup default nightly\n```\n\n## Example\n\nBasic CRUD operations with doublets:\n\n```rust\nuse doublets::{mem, unit, Doublets, DoubletsExt, Link, Links};\nuse data::Flow;\n\nfn main() -> Result<(), doublets::Error<usize>> {\n    // Use file as persistent storage for doublets\n    let mem = mem::FileMapped::from_path(\"db.links\")?;\n    let mut store = unit::Store::<usize, _>::new(mem)?;\n\n    // Create a point link (a link that references itself as both source and target)\n    // Point: 1 -> (1, 1)\n    let point = store.create_point()?;\n    println!(\"Created point: {}\", point);\n\n    // Create a regular link with explicit source and target\n    // Link: 2 -> (1, 1)\n    let link = store.create_link(point, point)?;\n    println!(\"Created link: {}\", link);\n\n    // The `any` constant matches any link in queries\n    let any = store.constants().any;\n\n    // Count all links in the store\n    println!(\"Total links: {}\", store.count());\n\n    // Iterate over all links using pattern [any, any, any]\n    println!(\"All links:\");\n    store.each_iter([any, any, any]).for_each(|link| {",

Highest priority (manually entered options)\n2. **Environment variables** - With case-insensitive lookup\n3. **Configuration file** - Dynamic config file path via CLI\n4. **Default values** - Fallback values\n\n## Languages\n\n### JavaScript\n\n[![npm version](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/lino-arguments.svg)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/lino-arguments)\n\nThe JavaScript version supports Node.js, Bun, and Deno runtimes.\n\n```bash\nnpm install lino-arguments\n```\n\n```javascript\nimport { makeConfig } from 'lino-arguments';\n\nconst config = makeConfig({\n  yargs: ({ yargs, getenv }) =>\n    yargs.option('port', { default: getenv('PORT', 3000) }),\n});\n```\n\nSee [js/README.md](js/) for full documentation.\n\n### Rust\n\n[![Crates.io](https://img.shields.io/crates/v/lino-arguments.svg)](https://crates.io/crates/lino-arguments)\n[![docs.rs](https://img.shields.io/docsrs/lino-arguments)](https://docs.rs/lino-arguments)\n\nThe Rust version uses clap for argument parsing.\n\n```toml\n[dependencies]\nlino-arguments = \"0.2\"\n```\n\n```rust\nuse clap::Parser;\nuse lino_arguments::{getenv_int, getenv_bool};\n\n#[derive(Parser)]\nstruct Args {\n    #[arg(short, long, default_value_t = getenv_int(\"PORT\", 3000) as u16)]\n    port: u16,\n}\n```\n\nSee [rust/README.md](rust/) for full documentation.\n\n## Repository Structure\n\n```\nlino-arguments/\n├── js/                    # JavaScript implementation\n│   ├── src/              # Source code\n│   ├── tests/            # Tests\n│   ├── examples/         # Usage examples\n│   ├── .changeset/       # Changesets for JS releases\n│   └── package.json      # npm package config\n├── rust/                  # Rust implementation\n│   ├── src/              # Source code",

Currently supports Telegram channels, VK walls, and X.com (Twitter).\n\n## Features\n\n- 🚀 **Fast & Modern**: Built with Bun.sh for lightning-fast execution\n- 📱 **Multi-Platform**: Send to Telegram channels, VK walls, and X.com\n- 🔧 **Configurable**: Environment-based configuration\n- 🏗️ **Modular Architecture**: Separate broadcaster implementations for easy extensibility\n- 📝 **Flexible**: Support for HTML formatting in Telegram\n- 🧪 **Testing**: Built-in connectivity testing\n- 🔍 **Logging**: Configurable logging levels\n- ⚡ **CLI-Friendly**: Easy to use command-line interface with yargs\n- 🤖 **Dual Telegram Auth**: Supports both Bot API and User Client authentication\n- 🔐 **Session Management**: Automatic session persistence for user authentication\n\n## Architecture\n\nThe tool follows a modular architecture with complete independence between components:\n- **[`broadcast.mjs`](broadcast.mjs)** - Main CLI entry point using yargs\n- **[`logger.mjs`](logger.mjs)** - Shared logging utility\n- **[`telegram.mjs`](telegram.mjs)** - Telegram broadcaster with TelegramConfig class\n- **[`vk.mjs`](vk.mjs)** - VK broadcaster with VKConfig class\n- **[`x.mjs`](x.mjs)** - X.com broadcaster with XConfig class\n\nEach broadcaster is completely independent and manages its own configuration using the `getenv` package. No shared configuration dependencies exist between platforms.\n\n## Telegram Authentication\n\nThe Telegram broadcaster supports two authentication methods:\n\n### Bot API Authentication (Recommended)\n- **Use case**: Official channels, groups, automated posting\n- **Setup**: Create bot via [@BotFather](https://t.me/botfather)\n- **Requirements**: `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` + `TELEGRAM_CHANNEL_ID`\n- **Permissions**: Bot must be admin in target channel\n\n### User Client Authentication (Advanced)\n- **Use case**: Personal messaging, advanced Telegram features\n- **Setup**: Create app at [my.telegram.org/apps](https://my.telegram.org/apps)\n- **Requirements**: `TELEGRAM_USER_BOT_*` variables\n- **Session**: Automatically managed with `.telegram_session` file\n- **Interactive**: May prompt for phone verification on first run\n\nThe broadcaster automatically chooses the available method, preferring Bot API when both are configured.\n\n## VK Support\n\nThe VK broadcaster supports both public groups and personal pages:\n\n### For VK Groups (Communities):\n```bash\nVK_OWNER_ID=-123456789    # Negative group ID\nVK_ACCESS_TOKEN=your_group_admin_token\n```\n- Requires admin rights to the group\n- Posts appear on the group's wall\n- Token needs `wall` scope\n\n### For Personal Pages:\n```bash\nVK_OWNER_ID=123456789     # Positive user ID\nVK_ACCESS_TOKEN=your_personal_token\n```\n- Posts to your personal wall\n- Token needs `wall` scope for your account\n- Must be a public profile for external posting\n\n## X.com Support\n\nThe X.com broadcaster supports multiple authentication methods with automatic prioritization:\n\n**Authentication Priority Order:**\n1. **Client ID/Secret + Access Tokens** (if configured) - highest priority\n2. **API Key/Secret + Access Tokens** (if configured) - fallback\n3. **Bearer Token** (if configured) - lowest priority",

[2026-05-01T08:43:24.050Z] [INFO]         "content": "name:\tlink-foundation/browser-commander\ndescription:\tA library simplify control of local browsers to e2e testing\n--\n# Browser Commander\n\nA universal browser automation library with a unified API across multiple browser engines and programming languages. The key focus is on **stoppable page triggers** - ensuring automation logic is properly mounted/unmounted during page navigation.\n\n## Available Implementations\n\n| Language | Package | Status |\n|----------|---------|--------|\n| JavaScript/TypeScript | [browser-commander](https://www.npmjs.com/package/browser-commander) | [![npm](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/browser-commander)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/browser-commander) |\n| Rust | [browser-commander](https://crates.io/crates/browser-commander) | [![crates.io](https://img.shields.io/crates/v/browser-commander)](https://crates.io/crates/browser-commander) |\n| Python | [browser-commander](https://pypi.org/project/browser-commander/) | [![PyPI](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/browser-commander)](https://pypi.org/project/browser-commander/) |\n\n## Core Concept: Page State Machine\n\nBrowser Commander manages the browser as a state machine with two states:\n\n```\n+------------------+                      +------------------+\n|                  |   navigation start   |                  |\n|  WORKING STATE   | -------------------> |  LOADING STATE   |\n|  (action runs)   |                      |  (wait only)     |\n|                  |   <-----------------  |                  |\n+------------------+     page ready       +------------------+\n```\n\n**LOADING STATE**: Page is loading. Only waiting/tracking operations are allowed. No automation logic runs.\n\n**WORKING STATE**: Page is fully loaded (30 seconds of network idle). Page triggers can safely interact with DOM.\n\n## Page Trigger Lifecycle\n\nThe library provides a guarantee when navigation is detected:\n\n1. **Action is signaled to stop** (AbortController.abort())\n2. **Wait for action to finish** (up to 10 seconds for graceful cleanup)\n3. **Only then start waiting for page load**\n\nThis ensures:\n\n- No DOM operations on stale/loading pages\n- Actions can do proper cleanup (clear intervals, save state)\n- No race conditions between action and navigation\n\n## Getting Started\n\nFor installation and usage instructions, see the documentation for your preferred language:\n\n- **JavaScript/TypeScript**: See [js/README.md](js/README.md)\n- **Rust**: See [rust/README.md](rust/README.md)\n- **Python**: See [python/README.md](python/README.md)\n\n## Architecture\n\nSee [js/src/ARCHITECTURE.md](js/src/ARCHITECTURE.md) for detailed architecture documentation.\n\n## License",

coverage**: Automated coverage reports with cargo-llvm-cov and Codecov\n- **Release automation**: Automatic GitHub releases and crates.io publishing\n- **Template-safe defaults**: CI/CD skips publishing when package name is `example-sum-package-name`\n\n## Quick Start\n\n### Using This Template\n\n1. Click \"Use this template\" on GitHub to create a new repository\n2. Clone your new repository\n3. Update `Cargo.toml`:\n   - Change `name` from `example-sum-package-name` to your package name\n   - Update `description`, `repository`, and `documentation` URLs\n   - Update `[lib]` name and `[[bin]]` name\n4. Update imports in `src/main.rs`, `tests/`, and `examples/`\n5. Build and start developing!\n\n### Development Setup\n\n```bash\n# Clone the repository\ngit clone https://github.com/link-foundation/rust-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template.git\ncd rust-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template\n\n# Build the project\ncargo build\n\n# Run tests\ncargo test\n\n# Run the CLI binary\ncargo run -- --a 3 --b 7\n\n# Run an example\ncargo run --example basic_usage\n```",
