Acts as a JSON/XML alternative for describing data via references.\n*Relevance:* Could be the canonical, language-agnostic on-disk format for storing CRM contacts/messages portably across runtimes.\n\n**2. link-foundation/lino-objects-codec** — Language: JavaScript (also Python/Rust/C#)\nUniversal serialization library encoding objects to/from Links Notation; supports circular references, object identity, UTF-8 (base64), and primitives across all four languages with feature parity.\n*Relevance:* Drop-in encoder/decoder to persist structured contact/message graphs (with cyclic relations like \"knows/replies\") in lino files.\n\n**3. link-foundation/link-cli** — Language: C#\nA CLI tool (`clink`) for CRUD on links via a single Markov-style substitution operation; turing-complete, builds on associative theory and a C# links data store.\n*Relevance:* Provides command-line manipulation/migration of the local data store from shell scripts during imports/exports.\n\n**4. linksplatform/doublets-rs** — Language: Rust\nRust implementation of \"Doublets\" — file-mapped associative storage where each link has Index/Source/Target. Supports memory-mapped persistence, generic integer types, thread-safe access, query patterns, and FFI bindings.\n*Relevance:* High-performance local-first storage engine for the entire personal data graph; persists to disk with no server.\n\n**5. linksplatform/doublets-web** — Language: Rust (WASM/JS bindings)\nWebAssembly bindings for `doublets-rs`, published to npm as `doublets-web`. Lets browsers/Node use the same Doublets store.\n*Relevance:* Enables a fully in-browser personal data store (no backend) — ideal for privacy-first PWA/Electron deployments.\n\n**6. link-foundation/lino-arguments** — Language: JavaScript + Rust\nUnified config library merging CLI args + env vars + config file (with case-insensitive lookup) using yargs (JS) / clap (Rust).\n*Relevance:* Standardizes CLI configuration across all importer tools (vk-export, telegram-bot, etc.) consistently.\n\n## Cross-platform SDK\n\n**7. deep-foundation/sdk** — Language: JavaScript\nA Next.js boilerplate/template for \"deep\" applications, mergeable into existing repos via a special \"ours\" git strategy; Collaboration\",\"url\":\"https://www.inkandswitch.com/pushpin/\"}]\n\nBased on the web search results, here's what I found about local-first software, Ink & Switch, Martin Kleppmann, and CRDT principles:\n\n## Local-First Software\n\nMartin Kleppmann, along with Adam Wiggins, Peter van Hardenberg, and Mark McGranaghan, published \"Local-first software: you own your data, in spite of the cloud\" as a major research contribution. Local-first software is a model where the copy on your laptop or phone is the primary copy and the cloud is demoted to a sync relay.\n\n## Ink & Switch\n\nMartin Kleppmann and team developed local-first software prototypes at the Ink & Switch research lab over the course of several years. Ink & Switch has developed an open-source, JavaScript CRDT implementation called Automerge, based on their earlier research on JSON CRDTs.\n\n## CRDT Principles\n\nConflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs) are data structures that are multi-user from the ground up while also being fundamentally local and private. CRDTs have the potential to be a foundational technology for realizing local-first software.\n\nCRDTs are general-purpose data structures, like hash maps and lists, but the special thing about them is that they are multi-user from the ground up. Users can view and modify the application state on their local device, even while offline, and the CRDT keeps track of any changes that are made, syncing the changes with other devices in the background when a network connection is available.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

Automerge offers familiar JSON document structures with multi-language support but historically had performance issues.\n\n**Performance & Scalability:**\nYjs is the fastest CRDT implementation by far. Yjs is a high-performance CRDT library that supports real-time collaboration with a memory-efficient, binary-based approach, and can handle large documents better than Automerge due to its garbage collection mechanism and binary encoding.\n\n**Algorithm Approaches:**\nAutomerge uses RGA (Replicated Growable Array) where each character gets a unique ID, while Yjs uses YATA (Yet Another Transformation Approach) optimized for sequential inserts with a linked list structure.\n\n## Use Case Recommendations\n\nYjs excels at text collaboration with modular architecture but requires custom UI development, and is good for teams building text editors or document collaboration who need granular control over synchronization protocols.\n\nFor developers choosing between them, Automerge's JSON-based nature offers a less steep learning curve than Yjs-specific functions.\n\n## Local-First Capabilities\n\nBoth libraries support offline-first workflows. Beeper's original clients were all derived from the open source Element suite of clients, which they've extended over the years to better support their chat aggregation features and their focus on personal chat.\n\n## Matrix Bridges\n\nTo aggregate all your contacts on all your networks into a single chat experience, Beeper takes advantage of Matrix's ability to \"bridge\" other networks into the Matrix network. Whenever you connect one of your other chat accounts to your Beeper account, they're running a piece of software that bridges messages from that account into your Matrix account.\n\n## Open Source Approach\n\nWhen Beeper started building, they made the fundamental decision to open-source most of their backend code and provide the option to self-host. All of Beeper's bridges are open source and they maintain a library to easily build new ones.\n\nAny user can run their own bridge, connected to any network they wish, and integrate it with Beeper seamlessly with everything syncing across all devices.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

The tool's power is fully realized when semantic augmentation, data augmentation, and regex annotation features are used together.\n\n## Multi-Modal Synthesis Approaches\n\nRecent systems for converting natural language descriptions into regular expressions have achieved some success with short, formulaic text, but real-world regexes are complex and hard to describe with brief sentences, sometimes requiring examples to fully convey user intent. A framework for regex synthesis combines natural language and examples, where a semantic parser maps the natural language description into an intermediate sketch with holes for missing components, and a program synthesizer searches over the regex space defined by the sketch and finds a regex consistent with given string examples.\n\n## Example-Based Learning Approaches\n\nRegular expression inference is a supervised machine learning and program synthesis problem that takes a cost metric for regular expressions and positive and negative examples of strings as input. SplitRegex is a divided-and-conquer framework that synthesizes subregexes from split positive substrings and produces the final regex by concatenating synthesized subregexes while satisfying negative strings.\n\n## User Confidence and Interface Design\n\nParticipants reported significantly more confidence in synthesized regexes when given options to see additional examples and corner cases generated by REGAE, finding it easy to experimentally verify whether the regex is correct.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

It's a lightweight, zero-dependency fuzzy-search library written in TypeScript that works in the browser and on the server, and is designed for searching small-to-medium datasets on the client side. It uses the Bitap algorithm for approximate string matching — handling typos, misspellings, and partial matches out of the box.\n\n**MiniSearch:**\nMiniSearch is a tiny, lightweight, full-text, and powerful search library for JavaScript designed to run on both Node.js and the web browser. MiniSearch uses an inverted index for fast full-text search capabilities and supports features like phrase matching and custom scoring.\n\n## Performance Comparison\n\nFuse.js is performant for small to medium datasets but may experience slower search times with very large datasets due to its fuzzy matching algorithm, which requires additional computation. MiniSearch offers good performance for small to medium datasets, but its performance may degrade with larger datasets due to the overhead of maintaining the index in memory.\n\n## Use Case Recommendations\n\nChoose Fuse.js for its fuzzy search capabilities, which allow for approximate matching. Choose MiniSearch if you want a simple and efficient full-text search library that is easy to integrate and configure, as it is designed for small to medium-sized datasets and offers a balance between performance and ease of use.\n\n## Popularity\n\nFuse.js has 5,562,701 weekly downloads and 19,733 GitHub stars compared to MiniSearch which has 585,600 weekly downloads and 5,635 GitHub stars.\n\nIn summary, Fuse.js excels at fuzzy/approximate matching and is more widely adopted, while MiniSearch is better suited for traditional full-text search with exact and phrase matching.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

It can be used with Node.js for server-side applications or Electron apps.\n\n## Technical Details\n\nSQLCipher solves the problem of unencrypted SQLite databases by providing transparent, full-database encryption using AES-256, with queries working exactly the same while the underlying data is encrypted at rest.\n\nSQLCipher uses widely available encryption libraries like OpenSSL libcrypto, LibTomCrypt, and CommonCrypto for all cryptographic functions. The encryption algorithm is 256-bit AES in CBC mode, and when initialized with a passphrase, SQLCipher derives the key data using PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512.\n\n## Electron Integration\n\nSetting up SQLCipher with Electron requires some specific setup steps. The key to success is proper key management - generate strong keys, store them securely, and never hardcode them in your application.\n\n## Note on Libsodium\n\nWhile your search query mentioned libsodium, the search results focused on SQLCipher's use of OpenSSL and other cryptographic libraries. However, these APIs have a considerably different shape than sql.js.\n\nabsurd-sql demonstrates storing sqlite3 databases inside IndexedDB databases. However, the absurd-sql project uses SharedArrayBuffer + Atomics workaround and is not actively maintained.\n\nwa-sqlite was the first project to publish an OPFS storage option for sqlite3, offering multiple VFS implementations including MemoryVFS, IDBBatchAtomicVFS, AccessHandlePoolVFS, OPFSAdaptiveVFS, OPFSCoopSyncVFS, and OPFSWriteAheadVFS.\n\n## Storage Backends\n\nThe main difference lies in storage approaches: absurd-sql was a proof of concept for building a SQLite Virtual FS backend using IndexedDB, while newer solutions focus on OPFS. SQLite WASM can be fast but you have to initially download the full binary and start it up which takes about half a second.\n\n## Performance and Architecture\n\nThere are currently two implementation approaches: Asyncify (used by wa-sqlite) and SharedArrayBuffer (used by official SQLite OPFS VFS), where Asyncify makes the WASM bigger and slower, while SharedArrayBuffer adds requirements on where and how web assets are served.\n\nIf you can live with SharedArrayBuffer's COOP/COEP restrictions and OPFS browser support, the official SQLite implementation is recommended for official support and complete API, otherwise wa-sqlite remains an option.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

[2026-05-01T08:45:16.551Z] [INFO]         "content": "Web search results for query: \"Electron Tauri Capacitor cross-platform desktop browser app comparison\"\n\nLinks: [{\"title\":\"Electron vs. Tauri | DoltHub Blog\",\"url\":\"https://www.dolthub.com/blog/2025-11-13-electron-vs-tauri/\"},{\"title\":\"Tauri vs. Electron: performance, bundle size, and the real trade-offs\",\"url\":\"https://www.gethopp.app/blog/tauri-vs-electron\"},{\"title\":\"Tauri VS. Electron - Real world application\",\"url\":\"https://www.levminer.com/blog/tauri-vs-electron\"},{\"title\":\"Tauri vs Electron [2026]: 96% Smaller Apps, 1 Winner\",\"url\":\"https://tech-insider.org/tauri-vs-electron-2026/\"},{\"title\":\"Tauri vs Electron Comparison: Choose the Right Framework | by RaftLabs | Medium\",\"url\":\"https://raftlabs.medium.com/tauri-vs-electron-a-practical-guide-to-picking-the-right-framework-5df80e360f26\"},{\"title\":\"Tauri vs. Electron: The Ultimate Desktop Framework Comparison\",\"url\":\"https://peerlist.io/jagss/articles/tauri-vs-electron-a-deep-technical-comparison\"},{\"title\":\"Comparing Electron and Tauri for Desktop Applications\",\"url\":\"https://blog.openreplay.com/comparing-electron-tauri-desktop-applications/\"},{\"title\":\"Electron vs. Tauri: Which cross-platform framework is for you? | InfoWorld\",\"url\":\"https://www.infoworld.com/article/3547072/electron-vs-tauri-which-cross-platform-framework-is-for-you.html\"},{\"title\":\"Tauri vs. Electron: A Technical Comparison - DEV Community\",\"url\":\"https://dev.to/vorillaz/tauri-vs-electron-a-technical-comparison-5f37\"},{\"title\":\"Tauri vs. Electron: A comparison, how-to, and migration guide - LogRocket Blog\",\"url\":\"https://blog.logrocket.com/tauri-electron-comparison-migration-guide/\"}]\n\nI found comprehensive information comparing Electron and Tauri. Here's what the search reveals:\n\n## Key Architectural Differences\n\nThe biggest difference between Electron and Tauri comes from how they render the UI. The Electron framework comes with a full Chromium browser engine bundled in your application. In contrast, Tauri uses a library called WRY, which provides a cross-platform interface to the appropriate webview for the operating system. This makes Tauri apps far more lightweight.\n\nIn Electron, the main process runs in a Node.js environment. Tauri, on the other hand, uses Rust. All the framework code and the main process entrypoint are written in Rust.\n\n## Performance & Resource Usage\n\nThe performance differences are significant. The bundled Chromium in Electron adds 80-120 MB to every application before you write a single line of your own code. Tauri consumed an order of magnitude less memory at idle, around 30 to 40 MB compared to 200 to 300 MB in Electron.\n\nTauri applications are much smaller (as low as 3MB-10MB) since they rely on OS WebView, have lower RAM usage because there's no full Chromium instance, have faster startup since it does not need to initialize a full browser engine, and are more battery-efficient since fewer background processes are needed.\n\n## Platform Support & Consistency\n\nElectron uses Chromium under the hood so your user sees the same on Windows, Linux and macOS. Tauri on the other hand uses the system webview: Edge Webview2 (Chromium) on Windows, WebKitGTK on Linux and WebKit on macOS. This means Tauri uses different underlying web engines on each OS, and these platform variations become factors developers must manage during app development.\n\n## Developer Experience\n\nIn Electron you can import things like normal, and, perhaps most importantly, you can write your Electron-specific code in pure JavaScript. This is a huge bonus for Electron's target audience: web developers. With Tauri using Rust, this makes it a bit less accessible to the average web developer.\n\n## Current Adoption & Future\n\nWith Tauri 2.x now supporting iOS and Android alongside Windows, macOS, and Linux, it has evolved from a desktop-only challenger into a true cross-platform framework that competes on territory Electron cannot reach.\n\n**Note:** Your search query mentioned Capacitor, which is a mobile development framework, but the search results focused on Electron vs. Tauri for desktop applications. If you need specific information about Capacitor as well, I can search for that separately.\n\n\nREMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks."

[2026-05-01T08:46:16.817Z] [INFO]             "type": "text",
[2026-05-01T08:46:16.817Z] [INFO]             "text": "I have all the research I need. Let me compile the concise summaries.\n\n## 1. Local-first software principles\nThe seminal essay \"Local-first software: You own your data, in spite of the cloud\" by Kleppmann, Wiggins, van Hardenberg, and McGranaghan at Ink & Switch defines seven ideals (no spinners, multi-device, offline, collaboration, longevity, privacy, ownership). CRDTs are positioned as the foundational data structure for realizing these ideals. Reference essay: https://www.inkandswitch.com/essay/local-first/\n\n## 2. CRDTs: Yjs vs Automerge\nYjs (https://yjs.dev/) uses YATA + binary encoding and is the fastest CRDT for collaborative text; Automerge (https://automerge.org/) offers ergonomic JSON-document semantics with multi-language support. For a personal CRM, Yjs fits chat/text streams; Automerge fits structured records. Both are offline-first and embed cleanly in Rust/WASM and JS.\n\n## 3. Personal CRM / Knowledge Graph\nMonica (https://www.monicahq.com/, docs https://docs.monicahq.com/) is the leading open-source PRM (Laravel-based), self-hostable with no cloud dependency. For graph-style PKG, see also Logseq/Athens-style projects. Monica's data model (people, activities, reminders, debts) is a useful reference schema for the Sovereign import layer.\n\n## 4. Unified messaging (Beeper / Matrix)\nBeeper (developer docs https://developers.beeper.com/bridges) bridges WhatsApp, Telegram, Signal, X, LinkedIn, etc. into a single Matrix homeserver; nearly all bridges are open-source under mautrix and the Beeper bridge-manager. Element clients (https://element.io/) are the canonical Matrix UI fork base. Self-hosting Matrix + mautrix bridges is the most legitimate path to a unified inbox.\n\n## 5. Pattern editor UI (regex/PEG inference)\nREGAE (Glassman Lab, UIST'20, https://glassmanlab.seas.harvard.edu/papers/ips_augex_uist20.pdf) explores interactive regex synthesis with augmented examples and corner-case generation. Chen et al.'s sketch-driven NL+example regex synthesis (TACL, https://direct.mit.edu/tacl/article/doi/10.1162/tacl_a_00339/96479) and SplitRegex (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2205.11258) are the strongest research baselines. For PEG, the Ohm-JS playground (https://ohmjs.org/) is the closest production UI.\n\n## 6. Fuzzy search in JS\nFuse.js (https://www.fusejs.io/) — Bitap-based, zero-deps, ideal for small/medium personal datasets and reply-template search. MiniSearch (https://lucaong.github.io/minisearch/) — inverted-index full-text with prefix/phrase support. uFuzzy (https://github.com/leeoniya/uFuzzy via npm-compare) is faster on large lists. For a Sovereign reply editor, Fuse.js + MiniSearch hybrid (fuzzy ranking + token index) is the typical stack.\n\n## 7. Node editors in JS\nReact Flow (https://reactflow.dev/) — most-adopted React-only library, excellent rendering and viewport hooks. Rete.js v2 (https://retejs.org/) — framework-agnostic with built-in dataflow/control-flow engines, closer to n8n's execution model. Drawflow (https://jerosoler.github.io/Drawflow/) — minimal vanilla-JS option. Rete.js is the best fit when you need execution semantics, not just visuals.\n\n## 8. WebRTC P2P sync\nPeerJS (https://peerjs.com/) wraps WebRTC signaling with a clean API for data channels. simple-peer (https://github.com/feross/simple-peer) is a lower-level building block. automerge-repo (https://automerge.org/docs/repositories/) ships network adapters including WebRTC and is the recommended path for CRDT sync. RxDB's WebRTC plugin (https://rxdb.info/replication-webrtc.html) and GunDB (https://gun.eco/) are alternatives if you prefer DB-level replication.\n\n## 9. Messenger export formats / APIs\nTelegram has an official Takeout API (https://core.telegram.org/api/takeout) emitting JSON/HTML; Telegram Desktop also exports JSON locally. WhatsApp offers per-chat \"Export chat\" (txt + media zip) and the WhatsApp Business Cloud API for opted-in flows; bulk export is restricted. Signal's protocol forbids server-side export by design — only on-device backups (Android encrypted backup, Desktop's signal-backup-decode). MasterScrat/Chatistics (https://github.com/MasterScrat/Chatistics) parses Messenger/Hangouts/WhatsApp/Telegram exports into DataFrames — a useful import baseline.\n\n## 10. Encryption at rest (Electron / browser)\nSQLCipher (https://www.zetetic.net/sqlcipher/design/) provides transparent AES-256-CBC + PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512 over SQLite and works with node-sqlite3/better-sqlite3 in Electron. libsodium-wrappers (https://github.com/jedisct1/libsodium.js) covers symmetric/asymmetric crypto in browser/Node with a small surface. age (https://age-encryption.org/) is the right tool for file-level export bundles and key wrapping.\n\n## 11. SQLite/LevelDB in browser (WASM)\nOfficial sqlite3 WASM (https://sqlite.org/wasm) is the canonical OPFS-backed build — uses SharedArrayBuffer + COOP/COEP. wa-sqlite (https://github.com/rhashimoto/wa-sqlite) offers multiple VFS variants (OPFSAdaptive, IDBBatchAtomic) and works without COOP/COEP via Asyncify. sql.js (https://sql.js.org/) is in-memory only — fine for read-only embedded data. absurd-sql is now considered legacy. For LevelDB, level-js over IndexedDB (https://github.com/Level/level-js) is the standard.\n\n## 12. Electron + browser cross-targeting\nTauri 2 (https://v2.tauri.app/) is the strongest fit for a Rust+WASM project — uses system webview, ~3-10 MB bundles, and now targets iOS/Android too. Electron (https://www.electronjs.org/) remains easiest for pure JS teams and gives Chromium parity across OSes. Capacitor (https://capacitorjs.com/) targets mobile + Electron via plugin and lets you ship the same web build to browser, mobile, and desktop with a thin native shell — a good companion if the Sovereign UI must run unmodified in a plain browser tab.\n\nSources:\n- [Local-first software (Ink & Switch)](https://www.inkandswitch.com/essay/local-first/)\n- [Local-first software (Kleppmann publication)](https://martin.kleppmann.com/2019/10/23/local-first-at-onward.html)\n- [Yjs](https://yjs.dev/)\n- [Automerge](https://automerge.org/)\n- [Best CRDT Libraries (Velt)](https://velt.dev/blog/best-crdt-libraries-real-time-data-sync)\n- [Monica HQ](https://www.monicahq.com/)\n- [Monica Documentation](https://docs.monicahq.com/)\n- [Beeper Bridges Docs](https://developers.beeper.com/bridges)\n- [Beeper Open Source](https://developers.beeper.com/open-source)\n- [REGAE / Interactive Program Synthesis (UIST'20)](https://glassmanlab.seas.harvard.edu/papers/ips_augex_uist20.pdf)\n- [Sketch-Driven Regex Generation Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2205.11258)\n- [Fuse.js](https://www.fusejs.io/)\n- [JS search libraries comparison](https://npm-compare.com/elasticlunr,flexsearch,fuse.js,minisearch)\n- [React Flow](https://reactflow.dev/)\n- [Rete.js](https://retejs.org/docs/)\n- [PeerJS](https://peerjs.com/)\n- [RxDB WebRTC replication](https://rxdb.info/replication-webrtc.html)\n- [Telegram Takeout API](https://core.telegram.org/api/takeout)\n- [Privacy International — Telegram data guide](https://privacyinternational.org/guide-step/4770/guide-getting-your-data-telegram)\n- [SQLCipher User can use one or multiple messages, and fuzzy search to find candidates from the history, once found user can select to add them pattern to match wider range of messages.\\n- Reply message variation group editor, we should use fuzzy search to extract typical replies to the same question or message from the history of own messages, user can add manually as many variations as he wants.\\n- Automated scripts, that handle typical messages like hi/hello, thank you, and so on. UI should be similar to n8n, with dependencies between patterns and reply variations. That should visualize typical dialogs tree from history, so that user can configure how to automatically control multi stage conversations, when multiple questions need to be asked once previous answers are gotten. So routing talking, first contact, knowing each other can be automated. It should be run in fully automated more or semi automated mode. In semi automated mode user can see that system detected some condition it can react to, and either manually select reply variation or confirm random selections as it would in fully automated mode.\\n- We should be able to use pattern matching on multiple messages, to scan conversations for personal private facts we got to know about each user, to help the user remember all facts where discussed in the each conversation without looking too much at it, saving time. All personal data captured from conversation automatically should be shown in form `question - answer`, for both sides you and your chat partner. For chats with multiple users we should be able to see what questions was answered by each chat participant.\\n- CRM features like ability to see in one place what we know about our contacts/friends in one place, ability cross reference, intersect sets of chats, groups, communites, and facts to select target audience and send them all hi/hello or stickers, to start massive manual conversations with only relevant and target audience\\n- Ability to search locally for people, communities, companies, messages, chats, with configurable parameters\\n- Unified broadcasing UI to support public posting on walls/public posts in all services\\n- Profile sync in all services\\n- Resumes sync in all services\\n- External services support: VK, Telegram, X, WhatsApp, Facebook, Linked-In, career.habr.com, hh.ru, superjob.ru\\n- Fully open-source and public domain requiring no trust\\n- Every function and feature should be importable as npm library\\n- NPM library should contain CLI interface, and electron desktop interface, ability to start web server to connect to local storage\\n- All clients should allow sync of the data between them using WebRTC mapped to localstorage or any configurable endpoint, if user wants to host it in the internet to simplify the access from small devices like mobile phones, with ability to work with full local database in personal cloud.\\n- Docker microservice for WebRTC server and for web server.\\n- We should be able to do universal Links access interface in both server and client. On server it should combine access to both read and write at the same time to binary storage (link-cli/doublets-rs) and text storage (links notation).\\n- We should use JavaScript on server and client and all UI apps with React.js, with Rust + WebAssembly for heavy workloads.\\n- We should also have a version of server and microservice fully written in only Rust, but as well in JavaScript.\\n- Application should use all the best practices from https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines, https://design.google, and Microsoft design guildelines.\\n- The code should be written in simple way with no premature optimizations \\n- All code should be documented with automated API documentation generation\\n- We should cover all tests fully with unit, integration, and e2e tests using http://github.com/link-foundation/browser-commander\\n\\nFor all data exchanges and local text data-source (we should prefer indented format to minimize brackets/parentheses):\\n- [2026-05-01T08:47:28.531Z] [INFO]           "content": "# Case Study: Issue #1 — Prototype Version 0.0.1 (Meta Sovereign)\n\n**Issue:** [#1 — Prototype version 0.0.1](https://github.com/link-foundation/meta-sovereign/issues/1)\n**Author:** [@konard](https://github.com/konard)\n**Status:** Open / In Progress\n**Pull Request:** [#2](https://github.com/link-foundation/meta-sovereign/pull/2)\n\nThis case study compiles all available information about the issue, decomposes it into atomic requirements, surveys the existing libraries and tools that can help, and proposes a phased solution plan that fits the local-first / privacy-first design constraints stated in the issue.\n\nThe artefacts in this folder are:\n\n| File | Purpose |\n| ---- | ------- |\n| `README.md` | This document — case study analysis. |\n| `requirements.md` | Atomic requirements list extracted from the issue. |\n| `solution-plan.md` | Phased plan mapping requirements to concrete deliverables. |\n| `components.md` | Catalogue of upstream libraries / repositories referenced in the issue, with a short note on relevance. |\n| `external-research.md` | Summary of external (non-issue) research about local-first software, CRDTs, unified messaging, pattern editors, fuzzy search, node editors, WebRTC sync, encrypted local storage, Electron/Tauri, and related topics. |\n| `architecture.md` | Proposed high-level architecture, with the Universal Links access layer and Rust+WASM heavy-workload split. |\n| `data/` | Raw artefacts used to build this study (issue body, comments, future PR snapshots). |\n\n---\n\n## 1. Vision (paraphrased from the issue)\n\n> A **Personal Meta Profile Sovereign** system. Fully local, privacy-respecting. The user actually owns and controls their data about their network of contacts, connections, and partners — across every social/messenger/job network they use.\n\nThe system is simultaneously:\n\n- A **unified messenger** spanning VK, Telegram, X, WhatsApp, Facebook, LinkedIn, career.habr.com, hh.ru, superjob.ru.\n- A **personal CRM**: contacts, communities, group memberships, intersections, mass-personal outreach.\n- A **personal memory**: structured `question → answer` facts captured automatically from conversations.\n- A **conversation automation platform**: pattern editors, reply-variation editors, n8n-style dialog graphs.\n- A **portable data store**: dual binary (Doublets) + text (Links Notation) representation, regular backups, `.lino` import/export.\n- A **distributed but local-first runtime**: WebRTC sync between user-owned devices, optional self-hosted relay.\n- An **NPM library** that exposes every feature programmatically — CLI, Electron desktop, web server, browser.\n- A **pure-Rust stack** alongside the JS/Rust+WASM stack for performance-critical and minimal-trust deployments.\n\n## 2. Why this case study exists\n\nThe issue explicitly requests:\n\n> *We need to collect data related about the issue to this repository, make sure we compile that data to `./docs/case-studies/issue-{id}` folder, and use it to do deep case study analysis (also make sure to search online for additional facts and data), list of each and all requirements from the issue, and propose possible solutions and solution plans for each requirement (we should also check known existing components/libraries, that solve similar problem or can help in solutions).*\n\nThis document is the central deliverable of that request. The implementation of the prototype itself is the next phase, planned in `solution-plan.md` and tracked through follow-up issues.\n\n## 3. Method\n\n1. **Source extraction** — issue body, labels and metadata captured via `gh` CLI to `data/issue-1.json`. Comments captured to `data/issue-1-comments.json` (currently empty).\n2. **Requirement decomposition** — each bullet of the issue body was split into atomic, testable requirements in `requirements.md`. Each requirement is tagged so it can be referenced by future PRs and follow-up issues.\n3. **Component survey** — every repository explicitly linked from the issue was inspected via `gh repo view` to identify what already exists in the link-foundation / linksplatform / konard ecosystem. Results captured in `components.md`.\n4. **External research** — public web sources (no GitHub) consulted for areas where the issue does **not** prescribe a specific library: CRDT sync libraries, pattern-synthesis literature, node editors, WebRTC sync stacks, encrypted-at-rest options for Electron and browser. Results captured in `external-research.md`.\n5. **Plan synthesis** — `solution-plan.md` groups requirements into phased milestones: (a) prototype data layer, (b) importer corpus, (c) unified UI, (d) automation graph, (e) sync. `architecture.md` shows how these layers compose.\n\n## 4. Headline findings\n\n- The link-foundation ecosystem already provides **most of the data-storage primitives** the issue calls for: `links-notation` (text), `lino-objects-codec` (object↔text), `doublets-rs` (binary, mmap), `doublets-web` (WASM bindings), and `lino-arguments` (CLI/config). The prototype's Universal Links access layer can therefore be built mostly by **integration**, not from scratch.\n- Importers for VK and Telegram exist as standalone konard repositories (`vk-export`, `vk-browser`, `vk-bot`, `telegram-bot`, `follow`, `broadcast`). They can be wrapped as adapters under a common `MessageSource` interface — see `architecture.md`.\n- **Beeper / Matrix bridges** (`mautrix-*`) are the most legitimate path to a unified inbox spanning networks (WhatsApp, Signal, X, LinkedIn, Telegram, etc.) without violating ToS or building dozens of bespoke scrapers. The Sovereign client can run a self-hosted Matrix homeserver locally and import directly from it.\n- **CRDT sync** for the WebRTC-backed sync requirement is best served by `automerge-repo` (structured records) and/or `Yjs` (chat text). Both have well-documented WebRTC adapters.\n- **Encrypted-at-rest** for Electron is well-served by `SQLCipher`. In the browser, `sqlite3.wasm` over OPFS plus a userland encryption layer (libsodium) is the closest equivalent.\n- **Node editor** for the n8n-like automation graph: `Rete.js v2` is the closest match because it ships with a real dataflow execution engine, not just a visual canvas.\n- **Pattern editor**: there is no off-the-shelf component for example-driven regex/PEG inference. A small custom UI on top of `ohm-js` (PEG playground) plus example-driven heuristics is the realistic prototype path; the deeper synthesis work (REGAE / SplitRegex) is a research direction for v0.x→v0.y.\n\nThe complete reasoning — including library URLs and trade-offs — is in `external-research.md` and `components.md`.\n\n## 5. Constraints honoured\n\nThe proposed plan respects every non-negotiable constraint stated in the issue:\n\n- **Public domain / Unlicense** licensing across the project (already true of this template).\n- **No premature optimisation**; simple code that works comes first.\n- **Every feature is an importable NPM package** — the prototype repository is structured so each adapter, the storage layer, the CRDT sync, and each UI surface can be split into a workspace package as it stabilises.\n- **All tests cover unit + integration + e2e**, with e2e using `link-foundation/browser-commander`.\n- **Two stacks**: a JS+Rust/WASM stack (default) and a pure-Rust stack (server/microservice variant). The data formats (Links Notation, Doublets) are language-neutral, so the two stacks share the same on-disk artefacts.\n- **Best-practice CI/CD parity** with `js-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template` and `rust-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template`. This repository was created from the JS template; gaps relative to the Rust template will be tracked as follow-up issues.\n\n## 6. Next steps\n\nThe prototype implementation of `0.0.1` is decomposed into the milestones in `solution-plan.md`. The intent of the linked PR (#2) is to land **this case study** plus the package metadata changes that establish the repository identity (`name`, description, README header), so subsequent feature PRs can reference the requirements catalogued here.\n\nOut of scope for this PR (will be follow-up issues / PRs):\n\n- Implementation of any importer adapter (VK, Telegram, etc.).\n- Any UI surface (chat, operator, automation graph, pattern editor).\n- WebRTC / CRDT sync layer.\n- Pure-Rust port of the server.\n\n---\n\n## 7. References\n\nThe full bibliography of external sources is in `external-research.md`. Key entries:\n\n- *Local-first software: You own your data, in spite of the cloud* — Kleppmann et al., Ink & Switch, 2019. <https://www.inkandswitch.com/essay/local-first/>\n- Yjs — <https://yjs.dev/>\n- Automerge — <https://automerge.org/>\n- Beeper Bridges — <https://developers.beeper.com/bridges>\n- Telegram Takeout API — <https://core.telegram.org/api/takeout>\n- SQLCipher — <https://www.zetetic.net/sqlcipher/design/>\n- SQLite WASM (OPFS) — <https://sqlite.org/wasm>\n- Rete.js — <https://retejs.org/>\n- Fuse.js — <https://www.fusejs.io/>\n- React Flow — <https://reactflow.dev/>\n- Tauri 2 — <https://v2.tauri.app/>\n\nThe libraries that are already linked from the issue are catalogued in `components.md`.\n"

[2026-05-01T08:47:28.549Z] [INFO]     "content": "# Case Study: Issue #1 — Prototype Version 0.0.1 (Meta Sovereign)\n\n**Issue:** [#1 — Prototype version 0.0.1](https://github.com/link-foundation/meta-sovereign/issues/1)\n**Author:** [@konard](https://github.com/konard)\n**Status:** Open / In Progress\n**Pull Request:** [#2](https://github.com/link-foundation/meta-sovereign/pull/2)\n\nThis case study compiles all available information about the issue, decomposes it into atomic requirements, surveys the existing libraries and tools that can help, and proposes a phased solution plan that fits the local-first / privacy-first design constraints stated in the issue.\n\nThe artefacts in this folder are:\n\n| File | Purpose |\n| ---- | ------- |\n| `README.md` | This document — case study analysis. |\n| `requirements.md` | Atomic requirements list extracted from the issue. |\n| `solution-plan.md` | Phased plan mapping requirements to concrete deliverables. |\n| `components.md` | Catalogue of upstream libraries / repositories referenced in the issue, with a short note on relevance. |\n| `external-research.md` | Summary of external (non-issue) research about local-first software, CRDTs, unified messaging, pattern editors, fuzzy search, node editors, WebRTC sync, encrypted local storage, Electron/Tauri, and related topics. |\n| `architecture.md` | Proposed high-level architecture, with the Universal Links access layer and Rust+WASM heavy-workload split. |\n| `data/` | Raw artefacts used to build this study (issue body, comments, future PR snapshots). |\n\n---\n\n## 1. Vision (paraphrased from the issue)\n\n> A **Personal Meta Profile Sovereign** system. Fully local, privacy-respecting. The user actually owns and controls their data about their network of contacts, connections, and partners — across every social/messenger/job network they use.\n\nThe system is simultaneously:\n\n- A **unified messenger** spanning VK, Telegram, X, WhatsApp, Facebook, LinkedIn, career.habr.com, hh.ru, superjob.ru.\n- A **personal CRM**: contacts, communities, group memberships, intersections, mass-personal outreach.\n- A **personal memory**: structured `question → answer` facts captured automatically from conversations.\n- A **conversation automation platform**: pattern editors, reply-variation editors, n8n-style dialog graphs.\n- A **portable data store**: dual binary (Doublets) + text (Links Notation) representation, regular backups, `.lino` import/export.\n- A **distributed but local-first runtime**: WebRTC sync between user-owned devices, optional self-hosted relay.\n- An **NPM library** that exposes every feature programmatically — CLI, Electron desktop, web server, browser.\n- A **pure-Rust stack** alongside the JS/Rust+WASM stack for performance-critical and minimal-trust deployments.\n\n## 2. Why this case study exists\n\nThe issue explicitly requests:\n\n> *We need to collect data related about the issue to this repository, make sure we compile that data to `./docs/case-studies/issue-{id}` folder, and use it to do deep case study analysis (also make sure to search online for additional facts and data), list of each and all requirements from the issue, and propose possible solutions and solution plans for each requirement (we should also check known existing components/libraries, that solve similar problem or can help in solutions).*\n\nThis document is the central deliverable of that request. The implementation of the prototype itself is the next phase, planned in `solution-plan.md` and tracked through follow-up issues.\n\n## 3. Method\n\n1. **Source extraction** — issue body, labels and metadata captured via `gh` CLI to `data/issue-1.json`. Comments captured to `data/issue-1-comments.json` (currently empty).\n2. **Requirement decomposition** — each bullet of the issue body was split into atomic, testable requirements in `requirements.md`. Each requirement is tagged so it can be referenced by future PRs and follow-up issues.\n3. **Component survey** — every repository explicitly linked from the issue was inspected via `gh repo view` to identify what already exists in the link-foundation / linksplatform / konard ecosystem. Results captured in `components.md`.\n4. **External research** — public web sources (no GitHub) consulted for areas where the issue does **not** prescribe a specific library: CRDT sync libraries, pattern-synthesis literature, node editors, WebRTC sync stacks, encrypted-at-rest options for Electron and browser. Results captured in `external-research.md`.\n5. **Plan synthesis** — `solution-plan.md` groups requirements into phased milestones: (a) prototype data layer, (b) importer corpus, (c) unified UI, (d) automation graph, (e) sync. `architecture.md` shows how these layers compose.\n\n## 4. Headline findings\n\n- The link-foundation ecosystem already provides **most of the data-storage primitives** the issue calls for: `links-notation` (text), `lino-objects-codec` (object↔text), `doublets-rs` (binary, mmap), `doublets-web` (WASM bindings), and `lino-arguments` (CLI/config). The prototype's Universal Links access layer can therefore be built mostly by **integration**, not from scratch.\n- Importers for VK and Telegram exist as standalone konard repositories (`vk-export`, `vk-browser`, `vk-bot`, `telegram-bot`, `follow`, `broadcast`). They can be wrapped as adapters under a common `MessageSource` interface — see `architecture.md`.\n- **Beeper / Matrix bridges** (`mautrix-*`) are the most legitimate path to a unified inbox spanning networks (WhatsApp, Signal, X, LinkedIn, Telegram, etc.) without violating ToS or building dozens of bespoke scrapers. The Sovereign client can run a self-hosted Matrix homeserver locally and import directly from it.\n- **CRDT sync** for the WebRTC-backed sync requirement is best served by `automerge-repo` (structured records) and/or `Yjs` (chat text). Both have well-documented WebRTC adapters.\n- **Encrypted-at-rest** for Electron is well-served by `SQLCipher`. In the browser, `sqlite3.wasm` over OPFS plus a userland encryption layer (libsodium) is the closest equivalent.\n- **Node editor** for the n8n-like automation graph: `Rete.js v2` is the closest match because it ships with a real dataflow execution engine, not just a visual canvas.\n- **Pattern editor**: there is no off-the-shelf component for example-driven regex/PEG inference. A small custom UI on top of `ohm-js` (PEG playground) plus example-driven heuristics is the realistic prototype path; the deeper synthesis work (REGAE / SplitRegex) is a research direction for v0.x→v0.y.\n\nThe complete reasoning — including library URLs and trade-offs — is in `external-research.md` and `components.md`.\n\n## 5. Constraints honoured\n\nThe proposed plan respects every non-negotiable constraint stated in the issue:\n\n- **Public domain / Unlicense** licensing across the project (already true of this template).\n- **No premature optimisation**; simple code that works comes first.\n- **Every feature is an importable NPM package** — the prototype repository is structured so each adapter, the storage layer, the CRDT sync, and each UI surface can be split into a workspace package as it stabilises.\n- **All tests cover unit + integration + e2e**, with e2e using `link-foundation/browser-commander`.\n- **Two stacks**: a JS+Rust/WASM stack (default) and a pure-Rust stack (server/microservice variant). The data formats (Links Notation, Doublets) are language-neutral, so the two stacks share the same on-disk artefacts.\n- **Best-practice CI/CD parity** with `js-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template` and `rust-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template`. This repository was created from the JS template; gaps relative to the Rust template will be tracked as follow-up issues.\n\n## 6. Next steps\n\nThe prototype implementation of `0.0.1` is decomposed into the milestones in `solution-plan.md`. The intent of the linked PR (#2) is to land **this case study** plus the package metadata changes that establish the repository identity (`name`, description, README header), so subsequent feature PRs can reference the requirements catalogued here.\n\nOut of scope for this PR (will be follow-up issues / PRs):\n\n- Implementation of any importer adapter (VK, Telegram, etc.).\n- Any UI surface (chat, operator, automation graph, pattern editor).\n- WebRTC / CRDT sync layer.\n- Pure-Rust port of the server.\n\n---\n\n## 7. [2026-05-01T08:48:33.730Z] [INFO]           "content": "# Requirements — Issue #1\n\nThis document decomposes the issue body into atomic, testable requirements. Each requirement has a stable identifier (`R-…`) so future PRs and follow-up issues can reference it.\n\nThe grouping follows the structure of the issue itself.\n\n## A. Data layer (storage and exchange)\n\n| ID | Requirement | Notes |\n| -- | ----------- | ----- |\n| R-A1 | Import/export with the unified database via external services (API and bulk export archive files). | Covers VK takeout, Telegram export, WhatsApp `Export chat`, Facebook download-your-data, X archives, LinkedIn data export, plus any platform API. |\n| R-A2 | Import/export with the unified database via `.lino` files. | Reuses [`links-notation`](https://github.com/link-foundation/links-notation) and [`lino-objects-codec`](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-objects-codec). |\n| R-A3 | Unified database stored in **both** binary form and text form, simultaneously. | Binary: [`doublets-rs`](https://github.com/linksplatform/doublets-rs) + [`doublets-web`](https://github.com/linksplatform/doublets-web) + [`link-cli`](https://github.com/link-foundation/link-cli). Text: `.lino`. The two are kept in sync. |\n| R-A4 | Regular automatic backups of the unified database into a configurable archive target folder. | Periodic snapshots, retention policy configurable, format = `.lino` + binary doublets snapshot bundled (e.g. `.tar.zst`). |\n| R-A5 | Indented Links Notation is preferred over bracketed form for all human-readable data exchange. | Reduces parenthesis noise; matches `links-notation`'s indented dialect. |\n\n## B. Unified communication UI\n\n| ID | Requirement | Notes |\n| -- | ----------- | ----- |\n| R-B1 | Unified chat UI rendering chats from **all** supported external services in one place. | Quality bar: Telegram clients ([`drklo/telegram`](https://github.com/drklo/telegram), [`telegramdesktop/tdesktop`](https://github.com/telegramdesktop/tdesktop)). |\n| R-B2 | Auto-completion in the chat UI based on the user's previous outgoing messages. | Source = local message history, ranked by recency / fuzzy similarity. |\n| R-B3 | **Operator UI** that auto-switches between chats/contexts so the user can focus on one unread item at a time. | Inspired by [`link-assistant/operator`](https://github.com/link-assistant/operator) (DONE / NEXT card stream). |\n| R-B4 | Unified broadcasting UI for public posting on walls/feeds/public posts in all services. | Reuses [`konard/broadcast`](https://github.com/konard/broadcast) for the outbound side. |\n\n## C. Pattern matching and reply automation\n\n| ID | Requirement | Notes |\n| -- | ----------- | ----- |\n| R-C1 | **Patterns editor** — automatically infer / construct / simplify / generalise regular expressions and PEGs from one or more example messages. | Includes fuzzy search to find candidate messages from history. |\n| R-C2 | Reply-message variation editor with fuzzy-search-driven extraction of typical replies to a question. | User can manually add as many variations as desired. |\n| R-C3 | **Automated dialog scripts** rendered as an n8n-style node graph linking patterns to reply variations. | Visualises dialog tree from history; supports multi-stage flows (greet → small-talk → ask). |\n| R-C4 | Two automation modes: fully automated and semi-automated (operator confirms / overrides each detected reaction). | Manual select of variation, confirm random selection, or override. |\n| R-C5 | Pattern matching across **multiple messages** to extract personal facts about each conversation partner. | Output: `question → answer` pairs, attributed per participant in group chats. |\n\n## D. Personal CRM features\n\n| ID | Requirement | Notes |\n| -- | ----------- | ----- |\n| R-D1 | One place to view everything known about a contact (chats, group memberships, communities, extracted facts). | The contact-detail page. |\n| R-D2 | Cross-reference / intersect sets of chats, groups, communities, and facts to define a target audience. | E.g. \"people who are in group X **and** mentioned topic Y\". |\n| R-D3 | Mass-personal outreach to a target audience (start manual conversations with a templated greeting / sticker). | Each conversation is still 1:1; the audience filter just batches the kick-off. |\n| R-D4 | Configurable local search for people, communities, companies, messages, chats. | Field selection, fuzzy matching, time range, network filter. |\n| R-D5 | Profile sync across all connected services. | Push profile updates to every service from one place. |\n| R-D6 | Resume sync across job-board services (career.habr.com, hh.ru, superjob.ru, LinkedIn). | Same idea as profile sync but for CV. |\n\n## E. External-service connectors\n\n| ID | Requirement | Notes |\n| -- | ----------- | ----- |\n| R-E1 | Native support for **VK**. | Reuse [`konard/vk`](https://github.com/konard/vk), [`konard/vk-bot`](https://github.com/konard/vk-bot), [`konard/vk-export`](https://github.com/konard/vk-export), [`konard/vk-browser`](https://github.com/konard/vk-browser), [`konard/follow`](https://github.com/konard/follow). |\n| R-E2 | Native support for **Telegram**. | Reuse [`konard/telegram-bot`](https://github.com/konard/telegram-bot), [`konard/follow`](https://github.com/konard/follow), [`konard/telegramify-markdown`](https://github.com/konard/telegramify-markdown). |\n| R-E3 | Native support for **X**. | Outbound covered by [`konard/broadcast`](https://github.com/konard/broadcast). Inbound = X archive import. |\n| R-E4 | Native support for **WhatsApp**. | Inbound = \"Export chat\" archives + WhatsApp Business Cloud API for opted-in flows. |\n| R-E5 | Native support for **Facebook**. | Inbound = Facebook download-your-data archive. |\n| R-E6 | Native support for **LinkedIn**. | Inbound = LinkedIn data export. |\n| R-E7 | Native support for **career.habr.com**. | Resume + job applications sync. |\n| R-E8 | Native support for **hh.ru**. | Resume + job applications sync. |\n| R-E9 | Native support for **superjob.ru**. | Resume + job applications sync. |\n\n## F. Distribution, sync, and deployment\n\n| ID | Requirement | Notes |\n| -- | ----------- | ----- |\n| R-F1 | Every function and feature must be importable as an NPM library. | Implies a workspace / monorepo layout once the prototype grows. |\n| R-F2 | The published NPM library ships a **CLI** interface. | CLI args use [`lino-arguments`](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-arguments). |\n| R-F3 | The published NPM library ships an **Electron desktop** interface. | One desktop binary per OS. |\n| R-F4 | The published NPM library can start a **local web server** that connects to local storage. | Browser tab connects to it. |\n| R-F5 | All clients (desktop / web / mobile) sync via **WebRTC**, mapped onto local storage **or** a configurable endpoint. | If user opts in, can host a personal cloud to ease access from mobile. |\n| R-F6 | A **Docker microservice** for the WebRTC server is provided. | Optional — users can run it on a VPS. |\n| R-F7 | A **Docker microservice** for the web server is provided. | Optional — same idea. |\n| R-F8 | A **universal Links access interface** is exposed in both server and client. | On client = local cache + direct server access. On server = simultaneous read/write to binary (Doublets) and text (Links Notation) storage. |\n\n## G. Stack constraints\n\n| ID | Requirement | Notes |\n| -- | ----------- | ----- |\n| R-G1 | Default stack: JavaScript on server and client + React.js for UI + Rust + WebAssembly for heavy workloads. | Heavy workload = pattern matching, full-text search, doublets I/O. |\n| R-G2 | Alternative stack: server + microservice fully written in **Rust**. | Complete parity for users who prefer minimum JS surface. |\n| R-G3 | Cross-platform packaging (iOS, Android, Electron) uses [`deep-foundation/sdk`](https://github.com/deep-foundation/sdk). | The issue lists this as the chosen base. Capacitor / Tauri are alternatives if SDK proves insufficient. |\n\n## H. Quality, design, and process\n\n| ID | Requirement | Notes |\n| -- | ----------- | ----- |\n| R-H1 | UI follows the best practices of [Apple HIG](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines), [Google Material](https://design.google), and Microsoft's design guidelines. | Documented design checklist per surface. |\n| R-H2 | Code is written in a simple way; no premature optimisations. | Optimise only after measurement. |\n| R-H3 | All code is documented with **automated** API documentation generation. | TypeDoc for JS/TS, rustdoc for Rust. |\n| R-H4 | Test coverage spans unit, integration, and e2e. | E2e uses [`browser-commander`](https://github.com/link-foundation/browser-commander). |\n| R-H5 | CI/CD parity with [`js-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template`](https://github.com/link-foundation/js-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template) and [`rust-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template`](https://github.com/link-foundation/rust-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template). | Any gap discovered while building this repo should be reported back upstream as a template issue. |\n| R-H6 | The project is fully open-source / public domain (Unlicense). | \"Requiring no trust.\" |\n\n## I. Documentation deliverable (this issue)\n\n| ID | Requirement | Notes |\n| -- | ----------- | ----- |\n| R-I1 | Compile data related to this issue into `./docs/case-studies/issue-1/`. | Met by this folder. |\n| R-I2 | Search online for additional facts and data; record findings. | Met by `external-research.md`. |\n| R-I3 | List every requirement extracted from the issue. | Met by this file. |\n| R-I4 | Propose possible solutions and a solution plan per requirement. | Met by `solution-plan.md`. |\n| R-I5 | Check for existing components / libraries that solve similar problems. | Met by `components.md` and `external-research.md`. |\n\n---\n\n## Requirements traceability\n\nWhen future PRs land features, they should cite the requirement ID in the changeset and PR description (e.g. *\"R-A3: doublets+lino dual-store skeleton\"*) so this list stays a live spec rather than a snapshot.\n"

[2026-05-01T08:48:33.751Z] [INFO]     "content": "# Requirements — Issue #1\n\nThis document decomposes the issue body into atomic, testable requirements. Each requirement has a stable identifier (`R-…`) so future PRs and follow-up issues can reference it.\n\nThe grouping follows the structure of the issue itself.\n\n## A. Data layer (storage and exchange)\n\n| ID | Requirement | Notes |\n| -- | ----------- | ----- |\n| R-A1 | Import/export with the unified database via external services (API and bulk export archive files). | Covers VK takeout, Telegram export, WhatsApp `Export chat`, Facebook download-your-data, X archives, LinkedIn data export, plus any platform API. |\n| R-A2 | Import/export with the unified database via `.lino` files. | Reuses [`links-notation`](https://github.com/link-foundation/links-notation) and [`lino-objects-codec`](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-objects-codec). |\n| R-A3 | Unified database stored in **both** binary form and text form, simultaneously. | Binary: [`doublets-rs`](https://github.com/linksplatform/doublets-rs) + [`doublets-web`](https://github.com/linksplatform/doublets-web) + [`link-cli`](https://github.com/link-foundation/link-cli). Text: `.lino`. The two are kept in sync. |\n| R-A4 | Regular automatic backups of the unified database into a configurable archive target folder. | Periodic snapshots, retention policy configurable, format = `.lino` + binary doublets snapshot bundled (e.g. `.tar.zst`). |\n| R-A5 | Indented Links Notation is preferred over bracketed form for all human-readable data exchange. | Reduces parenthesis noise; matches `links-notation`'s indented dialect. |\n\n## B. Unified communication UI\n\n| ID | Requirement | Notes |\n| -- | ----------- | ----- |\n| R-B1 | Unified chat UI rendering chats from **all** supported external services in one place. | Quality bar: Telegram clients ([`drklo/telegram`](https://github.com/drklo/telegram), [`telegramdesktop/tdesktop`](https://github.com/telegramdesktop/tdesktop)). |\n| R-B2 | Auto-completion in the chat UI based on the user's previous outgoing messages. | Source = local message history, ranked by recency / fuzzy similarity. |\n| R-B3 | **Operator UI** that auto-switches between chats/contexts so the user can focus on one unread item at a time. | Inspired by [`link-assistant/operator`](https://github.com/link-assistant/operator) (DONE / NEXT card stream). |\n| R-B4 | Unified broadcasting UI for public posting on walls/feeds/public posts in all services. | Reuses [`konard/broadcast`](https://github.com/konard/broadcast) for the outbound side. |\n\n## C. Pattern matching and reply automation\n\n| ID | Requirement | Notes |\n| -- | ----------- | ----- |\n| R-C1 | **Patterns editor** — automatically infer / construct / simplify / generalise regular expressions and PEGs from one or more example messages. | Includes fuzzy search to find candidate messages from history. |\n| R-C2 | Reply-message variation editor with fuzzy-search-driven extraction of typical replies to a question. | User can manually add as many variations as desired. |\n| R-C3 | **Automated dialog scripts** rendered as an n8n-style node graph linking patterns to reply variations. | Visualises dialog tree from history; supports multi-stage flows (greet → small-talk → ask). |\n| R-C4 | Two automation modes: fully automated and semi-automated (operator confirms / overrides each detected reaction). | Manual select of variation, confirm random selection, or override. |\n| R-C5 | Pattern matching across **multiple messages** to extract personal facts about each conversation partner. | Output: `question → answer` pairs, attributed per participant in group chats. |\n\n## D. Personal CRM features\n\n| ID | Requirement | Notes |\n| -- | ----------- | ----- |\n| R-D1 | One place to view everything known about a contact (chats, group memberships, communities, extracted facts). | The contact-detail page. |\n| R-D2 | Cross-reference / intersect sets of chats, groups, communities, and facts to define a target audience. | E.g. \"people who are in group X **and** mentioned topic Y\". |\n| R-D3 | Mass-personal outreach to a target audience (start manual conversations with a templated greeting / sticker). | Each conversation is still 1:1; the audience filter just batches the kick-off. |\n| R-D4 | Configurable local search for people, communities, companies, messages, chats. | Field selection, fuzzy matching, time range, network filter. |\n| R-D5 | Profile sync across all connected services. | Push profile updates to every service from one place. |\n| R-D6 | Resume sync across job-board services (career.habr.com, hh.ru, superjob.ru, LinkedIn). | Same idea as profile sync but for CV. |\n\n## E. External-service connectors\n\n| ID | Requirement | Notes |\n| -- | ----------- | ----- |\n| R-E1 | Native support for **VK**. | Reuse [`konard/vk`](https://github.com/konard/vk), [`konard/vk-bot`](https://github.com/konard/vk-bot), [`konard/vk-export`](https://github.com/konard/vk-export), [`konard/vk-browser`](https://github.com/konard/vk-browser), [`konard/follow`](https://github.com/konard/follow). |\n| R-E2 | Native support for **Telegram**. | Reuse [`konard/telegram-bot`](https://github.com/konard/telegram-bot), [`konard/follow`](https://github.com/konard/follow), [`konard/telegramify-markdown`](https://github.com/konard/telegramify-markdown). |\n| R-E3 | Native support for **X**. | Outbound covered by [`konard/broadcast`](https://github.com/konard/broadcast). Inbound = X archive import. |\n| R-E4 | Native support for **WhatsApp**. | Inbound = \"Export chat\" archives + WhatsApp Business Cloud API for opted-in flows. |\n| R-E5 | Native support for **Facebook**. | Inbound = Facebook download-your-data archive. |\n| R-E6 | Native support for **LinkedIn**. | Inbound = LinkedIn data export. |\n| R-E7 | Native support for **career.habr.com**. | Resume + job applications sync. |\n| R-E8 | Native support for **hh.ru**. | Resume + job applications sync. |\n| R-E9 | Native support for **superjob.ru**. | Resume + job applications sync. |\n\n## F. Distribution, sync, and deployment\n\n| ID | Requirement | Notes |\n| -- | ----------- | ----- |\n| R-F1 | Every function and feature must be importable as an NPM library. | Implies a workspace / monorepo layout once the prototype grows. |\n| R-F2 | The published NPM library ships a **CLI** interface. | CLI args use [`lino-arguments`](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-arguments). |\n| R-F3 | The published NPM library ships an **Electron desktop** interface. | One desktop binary per OS. |\n| R-F4 | The published NPM library can start a **local web server** that connects to local storage. | Browser tab connects to it. |\n| R-F5 | All clients (desktop / web / mobile) sync via **WebRTC**, mapped onto local storage **or** a configurable endpoint. | If user opts in, can host a personal cloud to ease access from mobile. |\n| R-F6 | A **Docker microservice** for the WebRTC server is provided. | Optional — users can run it on a VPS. |\n| R-F7 | A **Docker microservice** for the web server is provided. | Optional — same idea. |\n| R-F8 | A **universal Links access interface** is exposed in both server and client. | On client = local cache + direct server access. On server = simultaneous read/write to binary (Doublets) and text (Links Notation) storage. |\n\n## G. Stack constraints\n\n| ID | Requirement | Notes |\n| -- | ----------- | ----- |\n| R-G1 | Default stack: JavaScript on server and client + React.js for UI + Rust + WebAssembly for heavy workloads. | Heavy workload = pattern matching, full-text search, doublets I/O. |\n| R-G2 | Alternative stack: server + microservice fully written in **Rust**. | Complete parity for users who prefer minimum JS surface. |\n| R-G3 | Cross-platform packaging (iOS, Android, Electron) uses [`deep-foundation/sdk`](https://github.com/deep-foundation/sdk). | The issue lists this as the chosen base. Capacitor / Tauri are alternatives if SDK proves insufficient. |\n\n## H. Quality, design, and process\n\n| ID | Requirement | Notes |\n| -- | ----------- | ----- |\n| R-H1 | UI follows the best practices of [Apple HIG](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines), [Google Material](https://design.google), and Microsoft's design guidelines. | Documented design checklist per surface. |\n| R-H2 | Code is written in a simple way; no premature optimisations. | Optimise only after measurement. |\n| R-H3 | All code is documented with **automated** API documentation generation. | TypeDoc for JS/TS, rustdoc for Rust. |\n| R-H4 | Test coverage spans unit, integration, and e2e. | E2e uses [`browser-commander`](https://github.com/link-foundation/browser-commander). |\n| R-H5 | CI/CD parity with [`js-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template`](https://github.com/link-foundation/js-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template) and [`rust-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template`](https://github.com/link-foundation/rust-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template). | Any gap discovered while building this repo should be reported back upstream as a template issue. |\n| R-H6 | The project is fully open-source / public domain (Unlicense). | \"Requiring no trust.\" |\n\n## I. [2026-05-01T08:49:43.803Z] [INFO]           "content": "# Components & Libraries — Issue #1\n\nThis catalogue covers every repository explicitly linked from the issue. For each, we record what it does, primary language, and how it would be used in the Meta Sovereign prototype. Sources: `gh repo view OWNER/REPO` and the project READMEs.\n\nExternal (non-issue) references are in `external-research.md`.\n\n---\n\n## A. Data formats and storage\n\n### `link-foundation/links-notation`\n\n- **Language:** Rust (with bindings: JS, C#, Python, Go, Java).\n- **What it does:** Parses a custom *Links Notation* — e.g. `papa (lovesMama: loves mama)` — into lists of references and links and back. Acts as a JSON/XML alternative for describing data via references.\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Canonical text format for the unified database (R-A3, R-A5). All `.lino` import/export goes through this parser.\n\n### `link-foundation/lino-objects-codec`\n\n- **Language:** JavaScript (with feature parity in Python, Rust, C#).\n- **What it does:** Universal serialization library encoding objects to/from Links Notation. Handles circular references, object identity, UTF-8 (base64), primitives.\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Serialise the cyclic, multi-typed personal data graph (contacts ↔ messages ↔ groups ↔ facts) to `.lino` (R-A2) and back, without reference loss.\n\n### `link-foundation/link-cli`\n\n- **Language:** C#.\n- **What it does:** CLI tool (`clink`) that performs CRUD on links via a single Markov-style substitution operation. Turing-complete; built on the C# Doublets library.\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Command-line manipulation/migration of the local data store from shell scripts during one-off imports/exports (R-A1, R-F2). The Rust port (`link-cli` Rust variant or direct `doublets-rs`) would be the runtime equivalent.\n\n### `linksplatform/doublets-rs`\n\n- **Language:** Rust.\n- **What it does:** Rust implementation of *Doublets* — file-mapped associative storage where each link has Index, Source, Target. Supports memory-mapped persistence, generic integer types, thread-safe access, query patterns, and FFI bindings.\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** **Primary binary store** of the unified database (R-A3). High performance, no server needed.\n\n### `linksplatform/doublets-web`\n\n- **Language:** Rust → WebAssembly + JS.\n- **What it does:** WebAssembly bindings for `doublets-rs`, published to npm as `doublets-web`. Lets browsers/Node use the same Doublets store.\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Powers the same store **in the browser** (R-F4) and inside Electron's renderer when needed (R-F3). Enables a fully client-side personal data store.\n\n### `link-foundation/lino-arguments`\n\n- **Language:** JavaScript + Rust.\n- **What it does:** Unified config library that merges CLI args + env vars + config file (case-insensitive) using `yargs` (JS) / `clap` (Rust).\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Standardises CLI configuration across every importer / CLI subcommand (R-F2).\n\n---\n\n## B. Cross-platform SDK\n\n### `deep-foundation/sdk`\n\n- **Language:** JavaScript (Next.js).\n- **What it does:** A Next.js boilerplate / template for \"deep\" applications, mergeable into existing repos via a special \"ours\" git strategy; supports web, Electron and SSR builds.\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Starting template for the Next.js + Electron app shell (R-G3, R-F3, R-F4). If iOS/Android needs grow heavier than what this SDK covers, Capacitor (web→mobile) or Tauri 2 (Rust-native) are documented alternatives in `external-research.md`.\n\n---\n\n## C. Existing tools to reuse\n\n### `konard/broadcast`\n\n- **Language:** JavaScript (Bun).\n- **What it does:** Multi-platform message broadcasting CLI for Telegram channels, VK walls, X.com. Modular per-platform broadcasters and dual Telegram (Bot + User) auth.\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Outbound (export/post) side of the unified broadcasting UI (R-B4).\n\n### `konard/vk-bot`\n\n- **Language:** JavaScript.\n- **What it does:** Small VK bot using the Kate Mobile token auth flow.\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Reference implementation for VK token bootstrap and message handling loops (R-E1).\n\n### `konard/vk`\n\n- **Language:** Shell.\n- **What it does:** \"VK automation for personal auditory growth\" — shell scripts plus Selenium-based automatic VK token refresh.\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Headless token-refresh automation that can keep long-running personal VK importers authenticated (R-E1).\n\n### `konard/vk-export`\n\n- **Language:** Rust (with JS counterpart).\n- **What it does:** Parses exported VK private message HTML archives into JSON.\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Direct importer for migrating archived VK conversations into the personal CRM (R-A1, R-E1). Already Rust — fits the \"pure Rust stack\" goal.\n\n### `konard/vk-browser`\n\n- **Language:** HTML/JS.\n- **What it does:** Pure-browser React.js VK bot doing OAuth (Kate Mobile) + long polling, no backend; deployable on GitHub Pages.\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Demonstrates a fully client-side, server-less VK message ingestion path (R-E1, R-F4). Privacy-friendly because nothing leaves the browser.\n\n### `konard/telegram-bot`\n\n- **Language:** TypeScript.\n- **What it does:** Exports Telegram chat history (using a user account, not a bot) to Markdown + JSON + media files; partitions large exports; uses `lino-arguments`.\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Primary Telegram importer (R-E2, R-A1).\n\n### `konard/follow`\n\n- **Language:** JavaScript (Bun).\n- **What it does:** CLI tools to list/manage VK group chats and join Telegram groups/channels.\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Discovery side — enumerating user's group chats so they can be ingested (R-D1, R-E1, R-E2).\n\n### `konard/github-pages-telegram-mini-app`\n\n- **Language:** JavaScript (React + Vite).\n- **What it does:** Telegram Mini App template hosted on GitHub Pages, with Telegram SDK theme/user integration and Actions deployment.\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Optional starter for a Telegram Mini App front-end of the meta profile (auth, theming, mobile UI). Supplementary to R-G3.\n\n### `konard/telegramify-markdown`\n\n- **Language:** Python (originally TypeScript port available).\n- **What it does:** Converts standard Markdown to Telegram MarkdownV2, handling escaping, headings, lists, tables, code, spoilers.\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Render personal notes/exports back into Telegram cleanly when broadcasting/replying (R-B4, R-E2).\n\n---\n\n## D. UI quality references\n\n### `drklo/telegram` and `telegramdesktop/tdesktop`\n\n- The issue cites these two clients as the **quality bar** for the chat UI (R-B1). They are not dependencies — they're benchmarks. The interaction model (channel list, message-grouping, reactions, search, pinned messages, threaded replies) is what we replicate.\n\n### `n8n.io`\n\n- Cited as the **quality bar** for the automation node graph (R-C3). We do not depend on n8n; we build a similar canvas using Rete.js or React Flow (see `external-research.md`).\n\n---\n\n## E. Reference projects (UX inspiration)\n\n### `link-assistant/operator`\n\n- **Language:** JavaScript.\n- **What it does:** React UI concept for an \"infinite queue\" of cards/chats (humans + AIs from Telegram/VK/X) with two actions: DONE / NEXT, optimised for focus and decision-making.\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Direct UX inspiration for the operator/inbox view (R-B3).\n\n---\n\n## F. Testing\n\n### `link-foundation/browser-commander`\n\n- **Language:** JavaScript (also Rust, Python).\n- **What it does:** Universal browser-automation library with a unified API across engines focused on \"stoppable page triggers\" via a page state machine (LOADING / WORKING).\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Drives e2e tests (R-H4) and headless scraping for sites without official APIs (R-E5, R-E6).\n\n---\n\n## G. CI/CD baselines\n\n### `link-foundation/js-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template`\n\n- This repository was bootstrapped from this template (see `package.json` description and existing CHANGELOG).\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Source of all current CI/CD jobs (R-H5).\n\n### `link-foundation/rust-ai-driven-development-pipeline-template`\n\n- The Rust counterpart with rustfmt, Clippy, llvm-cov, fragment-based changelog, crates.io OIDC publishing.\n- **Use in Meta Sovereign:** Will be applied to the pure-Rust stack (R-G2) and to the Rust+WASM heavy-workload crate (R-G1).\n\n---\n\n## H. Summary table\n\n| Concern | Library / Project | Status |\n| ------- | ----------------- | ------ |\n| Text data format | `links-notation`, `lino-objects-codec` | Linked from issue |\n| Binary data store | `doublets-rs`, `doublets-web`, `link-cli` | Linked from issue |\n| CLI args | `lino-arguments` | Linked from issue |\n| Cross-platform shell | `deep-foundation/sdk` | Linked from issue |\n| VK ingestion | `konard/vk*`, `konard/follow` | Linked from issue |\n| Telegram ingestion | `konard/telegram-bot`, `konard/follow`, `konard/telegramify-markdown` | Linked from issue |\n| Outbound broadcast | `konard/broadcast` | Linked from issue |\n| Operator UX inspiration | `link-assistant/operator` | Linked from issue |\n| Chat UI quality bar | `drklo/telegram`, `telegramdesktop/tdesktop` | Linked from issue |\n| Automation graph quality bar | `n8n.io` | Linked from issue |\n| E2e tests | `browser-commander` | Linked from issue |\n| CI/CD baselines | JS + Rust ai-pipeline templates | Linked from issue |\n| Local-first sync (CRDT) | Yjs / Automerge | External research — `external-research.md` |\n| Unified messaging legality | Beeper / Matrix / mautrix | External research |\n| Encrypted-at-rest | SQLCipher, libsodium, age | External research |\n| Browser SQLite | sqlite3.wasm + OPFS, wa-sqlite | External research |\n| Node editor | Rete.js v2 (preferred), React Flow | External research |\n| Fuzzy search | Fuse.js + MiniSearch | External research |\n| Pattern synthesis research | REGAE, SplitRegex, Ohm-JS | External research |\n| Mobile / desktop | Tauri 2 (alt), Capacitor (alt) | External research |\n"
